- Ptarmigan The Snow Bird That Changes Color Arctic Wildlife Documentary: Kind: captions Language: en High above the frozen mountains, where the land stays white for most of the year, lives a bird so perfectly adapted to snow that it almost seems magical. This bird is called the ptarmigan. Known in the doer, the ptarmigan is one of nature's greatest examples of survival in extreme cold. In this video, we will explore how the ptarmigan lives, survives, changes color, finds food, escapes predators, and thrives in some of the coldest places on Earth. Stay with us till the end to discover why this bird is called the master of camouflage. What is a ptarmigan? The ptarmigan is a cold climate bird belonging to the grouse family. It is found in Arctic regions, tundra, and high mountain areas across North America, Europe, and Asia. Unlike most birds, the ptarmigan does not migrate long distances. Instead, it stays in freezing regions all year and survives harsh winters using special adaptations. There are three main types of ptarmigan. Rock ptarmigan, willow ptarmigan, white-tailed ptarmigan. All three species share similar survival techniques but live in slightly different habitats. The color-changing miracle. One of the most fascinating features of the ptarmigan is its ability to change feather color with the seasons. Winter, pure white feathers blend perfectly with snow. Spring, brown and gray patterns appear. Summer, dark brown colors match rocks and plants. Autumn, mixed shades prepare it for winter again. This natural camouflage protects the ptarmigan from predators like foxes, wolves, eagles, and owls. When the bird stays still, it becomes almost invisible. Where does the ptarmigan live? Ptarmigans live in some of the coldest and highest regions of the world. Arctic tundra, snowy mountains, alpine regions, polar plateaus. These areas experience extreme cold, strong winds, and limited food resources, but the ptarmigan is perfectly designed to survive here. Special body adaptations. The ptarmigan has several unique physical adaptations. Feathered feet. Unlike most birds, ptarmigans have feathers covering their feet. Acting like natural snowshoes, this helps them walk on snow without sinking and keeps their feet warm. Compact body. Their round body shape reduces heat loss, allowing them to conserve energy during freezing temperatures. Dense feathers. Ptarmigans grow extra thick feathers in winter, creating insulation similar to a winter jacket. Diet. What does a ptarmigan eat? Food is scarce in snowy regions, but ptarmigans are not picky eaters. Winter diet, twigs, buds, bark, willow branches. Summer diet, leaves, flowers, seeds, insects. Their digestive system is strong enough to break down hard plant material that other birds cannot eat. Surviving the harsh winter. Winter is the most dangerous time for ptarmigans. Temperatures drop far below freezing and predators are always hunting. Snow burrows. Ptarmigans dig small tunnels under the snow to sleep. Snow acts as insulation, keeping them warmer than the open air.

- Everything You Need to Know About Snowbird Retirement | Ep. 12: Kind: captions Language: en For a lot of Canadian retirees, there is a dream that comes with that retirement. It's sun, it's palm trees, it's the snowbird lifestyle where you spend part of the winter somewhere warm, [music] then you return home for the summer. Now, that all sounds great, but once you dig in, there's more to it than just packing your suitcase [music] and booking a flight. Welcome to episode 12 of Second Act. This is the series that we've designed to help Canadians navigate retirement with clarity and confidence. [music] Now, in the video today, I'm going to cover off the key financial and legal planning decisions behind the snowbird [music] dream. I'm going to look at why so many Canadians become snowbirds, the daycount and tax rules that you must follow, healthcare and travel insurance risks, the true cost of this lifestyle, renting versus owning a place down south, and crossber legal and [music] estate concerns. Thank you to Harvest ETS for supporting this entire series. I will be speaking more about them a little bit later in the video. Let's start with the obvious question. Why do so many Canadians choose the snowbird lifestyle? No doubt big part of that is escaping winter. You know, we've spent decades scraping windshields. The appeal of sunshine is pretty obvious, but there is more to the snowbird lifestyle than just warmer weather. Cold weather actually takes a toll on our aging bodies. Our joints, our energy levels, maybe even our mood in some cases, the shorter days, the longer nights, they can start to wear on you. Even simple errands start to feel like a bit of a chore. And then you retire and for the first time you control your own schedule. You don't have to ask anybody for vacation. You don't have to rush home on a Sunday night to be at work on Monday morning. You can finally ask yourself, if I could spend my time anywhere, where would I go? That is the true appeal of the snowbird lifestyle. It's the choice to live differently. Now, in this stage of life, time is your most valuable asset and where you spend it can make all the difference. [music] Let's look at that point where the snowbird dream bumps into legal reality. Because as tempting as it is to extend your stay down south, there is a hard line that you cannot afford to cross. Now, you've probably heard of the 183day rule. If you spend more than 183 days in the US in a calendar year, the IRS could consider you to be a US tax resident, even if you are a full-time Canadian citizen. That can open the door to things like US income tax filing obligations, possible double taxation on certain types of income, exposure to US estate tax even on your Canadian assets, and conflicts with your existing Canadian legal and financial structures. But here is where so many snowbirds get caught off guard. There is also what's called the substantial presence test. And this is a formula the IRS uses to total up your time in the US over 3 years. So it adds all of the days from the current year, onethird of the days from the previous year, then one sixth of the days from 2 years ago. And if that total hits 183 days or more, you could be deemed to be a US resident for tax purposes. And that is why tracking your days matters so much. You have to track them precisely. You can't round them. You can't estimate them. If you overstay by just a few days or a few weeks, even if it's due to illness, flight delays, you could face major tax consequences. If you're at all unsure, get professional advice. Talk with a qualified crossborder tax adviser who can help you calculate your day count, evaluate your risk, and walk you through options if needed. The US tax system is complex and when it comes to your residency status, it's always better to ask early than try and fix it later. Here's another area where a lot of first-time snowbirds get caught off guard. Every Canadian province has residency requirements for their public health insurance. Now, in most cases, you're going to need to be physically present in your home province for at least five to six months per calendar year in order to maintain your coverage. Now, this doesn't just mean having an address there. It means actually being there, and you're going to need your documentation to back it up if they ask for that. If you spend too long outside the country, your provincial coverage that could lapse, and getting it reinstated isn't always an automatic exercise. This is an obvious issue that creates real exposure, especially if you're dealing with chronic health conditions, prescriptions, or if you need care on short-term notice. Then there's the US health care system, which isn't cheap, and I'm putting that mildly. Even a short visit to an emergency room can cost thousands of dollars. A broken leg, a mild stroke, that could quickly run into five or six figures. It is the kind of thing that can derail a retirement plan literally overnight. So, obviously, medical travel insurance is non-negotiable, but this is what a lot of people miss. Not all policies are created equal. Before you buy, you're going to want to review coverage limits. Does the policy cover a million dollars, 5 million, or more exclusions, especially for high-risk activities or destinations. Pre-existing conditions. Now, some policies won't cover them or only after a short stability period. Deductibles. Know what you're going to have to pay out of pocket. and trip length factors in here. Many policies cap your coverage at 30 days, 60 days, 90 days and after that you may have to return home or reapply. Also consider emergency scenarios. If you are hospitalized, will you be treated there or will you be stabilized and shipped back to Canada? And here is a critical detail. If your health changes between now and your departure, a new prescription, a flare up, even just a test in many cases, you're going to have to update your insurer, or you may risk having them deny a claim. In just a few minutes, I'm going to look at the numerous costs that snowbirds need to be aware of. And one of those costs, of course, is travel. You've probably noticed travel isn't getting any cheaper. And when the prices rise, but planes, hotels, cruise ships are still packed, that tells you something about demand. And that's exactly what the Harvest Travel and Leisure Index ETF, ticker TVL, is designed to capture. This fund gives you exposure to a portfolio of global travel leaders, airlines, hotel chains, cruise lines, booking platforms, and entertainment companies. And these are household names that benefit directly from the rising travel activity and strong consumer demand that we're seeing these days. The fund is built around a focused index of leading travel and insurance companies. And for investors who want to generate more cash flow from the same group of companies, Harvest also offers TRVI, the Harvest Travel and Leisure Income ETF. It holds the same underlying portfolios as TRVL, but it uses an active covered call strategy to generate additional cash flow, which could be especially useful if you're drawing income in retirement. So whether you want exposure to travel sector growth or a higher income version of the same strategy, you can learn more about both of these funds at harvestf.com. Just scan the QR codes on your screen or click in the link in the description. Being a snowbird can absolutely be affordable, but we do have to keep it real. If you do not go into this venture with clear eyes and a well-built budget, then the lifestyle that was meant to bring you the freedom can quietly create a lot of financial strain. Let's look now at a few costs that tend to be consistently underestimated, especially by first- timers. Starting with travel, medical insurance. We covered the risks in detail just a few minutes ago, so I'm not going to repeat them here. But from a budgeting standpoint, this is one of the largest and most underestimated snowbird expenses. So just make sure that insurance is clearly built into your own snowbird budget. Secondly, currency exchange and fees. Every time you spend euros or US dollars using a Canadian credit card or convert money, there's a bike taken out by fees, exchange rates, or both. Now, over a few months, these small hits can add up. Think about it as well. If the looney dips, your spending power drops, too. So you should have a strong budgeting strategy in place that accounts for currency fluctuation risk. A lot of snowbirds set up a dedicated US dollar account to deal with this issue. Also consider transportation and travel logistics. Think of flights, rental cars, airport parking, extra luggage fees. There are a lot of little costs in between just getting there and back. And if you're a snowbird who likes to return home for the holidays, family events, medical appointments, then those repeat trips can quickly eat into your annual travel budget. Also, property related expenses. If you own a second home at your destination of choice, you have to think beyond just the purchase price. You will be responsible for property taxes, which are often higher for non-residents, HOA or park fees, maintenance and repairs, utilities and insurance, including special coverage for vacant periods. Even if you are renting, peak season prices can vary wildly by location and year, especially in higher demand areas like Florida or Arizona. Now, here's one that catches a lot of people offguard. Vacation mode spending, I'm going to call it. When you are in a sunny environment, meeting friends, going out more, sightseeing, spending can ramp up pretty quickly and you might not even notice it. You might eat out more, you might shop differently or book excursions that you normally wouldn't splurge on back at home. There's nothing wrong with that, but it does need to be part of your plan so that it does not get out of control. Bottom line here, you don't need to be wealthy to be a snowbird, but you do need a wellthoughtout, sustainable plan. The goal is to make sure that the lifestyle that you've dreamed of fits comfortably within your retirement and so that the pros always outweigh the cons. One of the biggest questions that regular snowbirds face is whether to rent a place each year or buy a second property. The truth here is that there is no one-sizefits-all answer. I want to start with the case for owning. Now, some retirees love having a permanent home base down south. Owning provides familiarity. You get to know your neighbors, your community, your routine. Consistency. No scrambling for a rental or wondering if prices will spike next year. Personalization. you can actually make your space truly yours. There's also potential savings over the long run, especially if prices rise. But owning also means you have tied up capital in a non-liquid asset, ongoing maintenance, taxes, insurance, and HOA fees, estate and crossber tax implications, especially if it's a US property, and a need to coordinate property management while you're away. So, if you are committed to a snowbird lifestyle and want to set roots in one community, owning can work. Just make sure that it aligns with your overall financial and estate plan. Now, I do want to look at the flip side. Renting does offer freedom, flexibility, especially in the early years. This is ideal if you just want to try out the lifestyle. If you want to test different locations before settling into one, if you'd rather avoid the responsibilities of home ownership, your health, your finances, family needs, they may change from year to year. One drawback is that rental places can fluctuate, and in peak months, demand is often very high, but renting does give you options. You aren't locked into a particular location or a community if it turns out that it's not the right fit for you. If you are new to this and still figuring out what works, it's probably best to start by renting. Think of it sort of as an extended factf finding trip. You're going to learn what size of home suits your needs, whether you prefer gated communities, RV resorts, beach condos, how accessible local healthc care, transportation, and amenities are, and how the lifestyle actually feels once the novelty wears off. After a season or two, you're going to be in a far better position to decide whether owning makes sense or whether the flexibility is still the right choice for you. Legal and estate planning is often a part of the snowbird list [music] that gets overlooked until something goes wrong. But if you own a property in the US or if you spend a significant amount of time there, then crossborder legal planning is essential. owning US property, even just a modest condo or a trailer, that could expose you to US estate tax. And this can apply even if you're a Canadian citizen and resident. The threshold for US estate tax is much lower for non-citizens than it is for Americans. And it is based on the value of your US situated assets at death. It doesn't matter if your overall estate is under the Canadian threshold. If the IRS sees you as having a taxable US estate, your heirs might be in for a nasty surprise. Now we should also talk about crossber wills and powers of attorney. Another important thing to consider here is that the standard Canadian will and power of attorney might not be recognized or enforceable in certain US jurisdictions. You may need a crossber will or at a minimum a US compliant will that addresses your US assets. Powers of attorney that meet legal standards in both countries for property and for healthcare. updated advanced medical directives that travel with you, especially if you're spending months at a time away. If you were incapacitated while you're abroad, your family will need documents that local hospitals and financial institutions actually recognize. Now, you also want to simplify things for your loved ones. Consider preparing an estate summary sheet. This is a single document that lists where your key assets are located, who your legal contacts are, what your wishes are if something happens, and how to access digital records and passwords. This doesn't replace legal documents, but it can help prevent chaos if your family suddenly does need to step in. Very, very important here. This is not DIY territory. The rules are complex. The consequences of doing something wrong or nothing is very expensive and can be stressful for your family. Again here, a good crossber estate lawyer can help ensure that your documents and your intentions hold up on both sides of the border. I hope by this point in the episode it is clear that snowboarding is a lifestyle choice and the people who thrive in it are the ones who actually treat it that way. They build in a rhythm between the two homes, two communities and two ways of life. They stay rooted in Canada. They maintain close ties here with your family, your friends, and the causes that you care about. At the same time, they do create meaningful routines and relationships wherever they land during the winter months. That could mean attending a local church or community center, volunteering part-time while you're abroad, staying active with things like golf, yoga, walking classes, dance classes, pickle ball, hosting friends or family for part of the season, or even continuing part-time remote work or projects that you are passionate about. There is something that these retirees all have in common and that is intentionality. They know that this is not a vacation. It's sort of a chapter of retirement that they are writing on their own terms. As I talked about way back in episode one of this series, retirement is a lot more than just not [music] working. It is about choosing how you want to live. And for a lot of Canadians, that snowbird life is a beautiful way to make the most of this chapter. This episode wraps up our second act series. 12 episodes that were designed to help Canadians transition into your own retirement with clarity, with confidence. - The real reason American health care is so expensive: Kind: captions Language: en I cannot tell you how obsessed I am with this chart. It shows exactly what is wrong with America's conversation about health care. On one level, you've seen this chart before. It shows health care spending as a share of the economy of a bunch of countries. There's Germany and France and Japan and Canada and oh! There's America. But now I want to add something you haven't seen to this chart. This is how much of that spending in each country is private and how much is public. Here's what's amazing: America's government spending on health care on programs like Medicaid and Medicare and the VA - our versions of socialized medicine. It's about the same size as these other countries. These countries where the government runs the whole health care system! And then there's our private spending. It's the private insurance system that makes health care in America so expensive. Conventional wisdom says that the government is more expensive than the private sector. "It can't say no. It's corrupt, it's inefficient, it's slow." "If you want something done right you give it to the private sector." That is what we hear in America all the time. And yet here we are with the biggest private sector spending the most. If you look at the data on physician visits and hospital discharges, you can get rid of one theory. Americans don't consume more health care than people in these other countries. We don't go to the doctor more than the Germans or the Japanese. In fact we go to the doctor less. The difference between us and them is that we pay more. Every time we go to the doctor for everything from an angioplasty to a hip replacement from a c-section to a pain reliever. In America, the price for the same procedure at the same hospital, it varies enormously depending on who is footing the bill. The price for someone with public insurance like Medicare or Medicaid is often the lowest price. These groups he covers so many people that the government can demand lower prices from hospitals and doctors and they get those lower prices. If the doctors and hospitals say 'No' they lose a ton of business. They lose all those people on Medicare all those people on Medicaid. But there are hundreds of private insurance companies And they each cover far fewer people than a Medicare or a Medicaid. And each one has to negotiate prices and hospitals and doctors are on their own. And if you're uninsured, you have even less leverage. Nobody is negotiating on your behalf. So you end up paying the highest price. One study found that most hospitals charge uninsured patients four times as much as Medicare patients for an ER visit. Other countries, they don't have this problem. Instead of every private insurance company negotiating with every healthcare provider. There's just this big list. The country, the central government, they go and they say, "If you want to sell to us, to all of our people, then here's what you can charge for a checkup. Here is what you can charge for an MRI or a prescription for Lipitor. And so then whether that bill goes to the heavily regulated private insurance companies in Germany or directly to the government like in the UK. Each country is telling the doctor or hospital or drug company how much that bill will be. And because the government controls access to all of the customers. It's an offer that hospitals and doctors and pharmaceutical companies typically can't refuse. "I'm going to make him an offer he can't refuse." In America the idea is that you'll be a consumer. That you'll do what you do when you go to Best Buy and buy a television. But that just doesn't work in healthcare. It doesn't work in healthcare because you often come and get health care when you're unconscious, in an ambulance, when you're scared, when it's for your spouse or your child It is a time when you have the least bargaining power. You are not usually capable of saying, 'No.' You're not knowledgeable enough to do it, you're not comfortable doing it, or you're not conscious enough to do it. That's why in other countries the government is a person who can say 'No' for you. You can say, 'No, that's too expensive you're going to have to lower your price' because they do have that power. Anchor: A new push for single-payer health care right here in the US. Demonstrator: What do we want? Crowd: Single-payer! Demonstrator: When do we want it? Crowd: Now! Anchor: California and others are saying maybe we should adopt the European model. Klein: If we decided to create a single-payer system with one of these huge price lists in the US There would be nothing to stop lobbying from hospitals from doctors from drug companies. And those prices would get influenced. So we could end up with a single-payer system that is expensive. Even as expensive as our current system. It all depends on how much you negotiate down the prices and now in America these groups have so much power because they are so rich. That it's really hard to get them to bring down the prices. This is the irony of American healthcare: It's so expensive that it's become hard to make it cheaper. All that money they make, that becomes political power. And years and years and years of overpaying - those are huge industries now. And they have a lot of influence in Congress. Under a single-payer system if we did drive prices down, doctors and hospitals they would be paid less than they are right now. That might mean some of them close or some go out of business or some move. It would be really painful. One person's waste is another person's essential service or local hospital or their income. But then single-payer it's not an all-or-nothing choice. For instance, there's a really interesting section of Bernie Sanders Medicare-for-all bill. Where he lays out this interim plan. It's a plan he wants while he's setting up his new single-payer system. And in that plan, he expands Medicare to cover vision and dental. And he opens it to nearly everyone. Not just people 65 and older. All kids go on Medicare automatically and most adults can buy in. That plan, on its own, it wouldn't get American health care spending far down overnight. But it would at least begin to recognize what we already know and what most other countries already do: That health care is one of those things the government can do cheaper and better than the private sector.

But Roseanne ended up in a Florida hospital where she received a life-changing diagnosis. But there was more shocking news. It turns out there was a clause in the fine print of her health insurance policy that proved costly. It was supposed to be a welldeserved vacation. Roseanne Timberl and her husband heading to Walt Disney World in Florida to unwind and relax. Well, Disney World is is a great place just to hang out. >> The trip was this past February, but just prior to leaving, Roseanne says she was experiencing flu-l like symptoms. >> I wasn't feeling the greatest before I went on vacation. >> As a precaution before her trip, Roseanne says she consulted with her doctor about her symptoms and had a blood test, but says at the time there was no cause for alarm. >> But I'm still able to go to work. So, if I'm able to go to work, I think I'm able to go on this vacation. Roseanne says she had extended health insurance coverage through her work and felt confident she was covered. But when she arrived in Florida, Roseanne says her symptoms were getting worse. Eventually, Roseanne decided to go to emergency. But before she did, Roseanne says she called her insurance provider to make certain her visit would be covered. >> The agent said, "Yes, you're covered and you do have extended health for out of country." and I said, "Okay, because if not, I'm jumping on the next plane." >> Roseanne arrived at the Florida hospital where she received shocking news. She was diagnosed with ovarian cancer. >> I guess it was um traumatizing. It's not something you would ever think that would happen on vacation. >> Roseanne spent 5 days in the Florida hospital undergoing further testing. When she returned home, her insurance provider denied her claim, stating, "The expenses incurred at travel were directly related to your unstable pre-existing condition." Within the fine print of her policy, there was a stability clause, which means a pre-existing medical condition must remain stable for 90 days prior to traveling. Roseanne was hit with a medical bill of $18,000 US. >> Just absolutely shocking. I just wish I'd gotten on the plane and come home instead of dealing with all that in the States. >> Travel expert Claire Nu says sadly Roseanne's claim denial isn't uncommon. Stability clauses are often written in standard medical insurance policies. >> A lot of people still don't realize that there is a stability period. And in some policies it's 3 months. In others it can be as high as six months. And it's really important that you read the fine print. A GoFundMe page has been set up by family and friends to help Roseanne. Still, Roseanne hopes her story helps others avoid the insurance nightmare she's still dealing with. >> Understand your policy very, very, very clearly. Um, because you're having to be your own best advocate. >> And we are told by Roseanne's insurance provider there is a resolution process to dispute her claim if she chooses. That process will allow her claim to be reviewed by a complaints investigator. Again, travel experts warn it's important to read the fine print of any policy, especially around the stability clause, and to ask questions. Also, make sure you request the actual policy. That's not a brochure. That's a full policy with full details of what is covered and what isn't. And if you have a consumer issue for me, you can email me at consumer matters@globalnews.ca. - What Does Life Feel Like in a Florida Manufactured Home Community?: Kind: captions Language: en By now, perhaps you are thinking a Florida manufactured home might be right for your retirement dream. But you're still left wondering, what does life in these communities actually feel like? You want to know more? Stay tuned. For a lot of people, retirement begins in the same neighborhood where they spent their years building a life. And for a while, that may feel perfectly natural. It's like a vacation. But then you begin to notice the shift. The kids are grown. Work is over. The routines that once brought people together begin to disappear. The neighborhood's still there, but life around it just doesn't quite feel the same. It's not that anything is terribly wrong. It's just that most of your neighbors are still socially connected through the people they see at work, through parenting, and through all the routines that still shape their days. Meanwhile, you may have a lot more time on your hands and a lot fewer built-in connections to share it with. Your schedule is just no longer in sync with the neighborhood. And life can get awfully quiet. Not peaceful necessarily, just disconnected. That's one reason people begin to ask whether there might be a better setting for this stage of life. Not because they need constant entertainment, but because they'd like ordinary life to feel more in sync with the kind of retirement they imagined. One of the big differences in a Florida manufactured home community is that life is easier to step into. You're not starting with a blank slate. You're coming to a place where people are enjoying a similar season in life. They're part of the daily rhythm that is more in sync with retirement years and the kind of life that many retirees are hoping to create. And that alone changes a lot. I can tell you from personal experience, when you come into a new community like this, your biggest challenge really can be remembering all of the names of the people you meet and what you talked about with each of them. In fact, I recommend carrying a little notebook or opening an app on your phone and taking a minute to jot people down, their names, maybe where they're from, maybe what activity you met them at, because you're likely to meet a lot of people in a short period of time, and that's a good problem to have. Simple things like an HOA coffee can quickly turn into invitations and opportunities through the people you meet there. A lot of friendships begin over activities like botchi or pickle ball. Others grow from helping out at community events or simply seeing the same friendly faces day after day. Before long, these small connections can add up to as full a social life as you want with good-hearted, like-minded neighbors, many of whom may soon become real friends. Now, another thing that makes life feel different is this. In a good community, activity is not something you have to build from scratch. It's already there. And this is where your choice of community begins to intersect with the kind of retirement life you want. The size of the community, where it's located, and the amenities it offers start to matter. In most communities, there's a pool and a clubhouse. You'll often find activities like botchi, shuffleboard, horseshoes, tennis, and pickle ball. There are usually card groups and sometimes even tournaments for games like Hand and Foot, Texas Holde, Uker, cribage, and more. Some communities have golf courses. Many also offer craft groups, travel groups, dinner parties, dance shows, lunchons, and seasonal events. In larger communities, the calendar can stay pretty full. And what matters is not that all these things exist. What matters is that they are right there, easy to access and easy to join if you want to. You can take part in as many or as few as you like. And the community you choose will have a lot to do with what's available. That changes your days from searching for something to do to choosing what sounds good to do today. Because when something is close by, it's much easier to say yes. You don't have to drive all over town. You don't have to organize everything yourself. You can simply step in as much or as little as you choose. And that's one of the things that can make retirement feel better. You're not spending your days trying to invent a life. You're stepping into one. To some people, that may sound too busy. But let me reassure you. Community living does not mean you have to be involved all the time. It's your day. You get to decide how to spend it. The beauty of a good community is that participation is never supposed to feel like an obligation. It's an option. Some days you may go to the pool and just sit for a while. Some days you may join a card game. Some days you may take a walk, chat with a neighbor, and call it a good day. Some days you may just stay home, enjoy the quiet, and never leave the house. That flexibility matters because retirement should not feel like one more schedule that you have to keep up with. It should feel like freedom. The real advantage is not that you must be busy. It's that life gives you something to say yes to when you want it. And that's a very different thing. There's also a practical side to this that often matters more than people expect. At my home in Connecticut, I had a swimming pool and I really enjoyed it in the summer, but it was a lot of work. Cleaning it, balancing chemicals, and staying on top of maintenance took time, money, and effort. today. All I have to do is walk over to the clubhouse and jump in. That's one of the real advantages of this kind of community living. A lot of things you enjoy are already there and they're maintained by somebody else. The pool is ready. The clubhouse is ready. The game room is ready. The common areas are cared for. The grounds are maintained. Things like tennis or pickle ball courts are right there ready to use. And not only are they convenient, they're also maintained for you by management. You get the benefit without carrying the burden. You're not just getting amenities, you're getting relief from responsibility. That means more of your day can be spent living and less of it spent managing. And when that starts happening day after day, retirement begins to feel very different. Another thing people notice is how much easier it can be to make new friends. Because many friendships earlier in life came from circumstance. You worked together. Your kids knew each other. You saw each other all the time. Retirement changes that. You can certainly go looking for connections in your old neighborhood through a local club, volunteer work, church, or other community activities. But there are often fewer natural places to meet like-minded people on a regular basis. So when people move into a place where everyday interaction becomes easier again, it can feel refreshing. You're no longer depending on chance to build your social life. You're living in an environment that gives friendship room to happen. That doesn't mean everyone instantly becomes close friends, but it does mean the environment often makes friendships easier to grow. You see the same people. You share a similar stage in life. You may have similar concerns, similar rhythms, similar interests. And because you're in proximity and in a place that encourages ordinary interaction, connection comes more naturally. And in retirement, that can make a real difference in how your daily life feels. There's also another layer to this, and it's an important one. It's not just that people are friendly. It's that many of them are at a similar stage in life. That means they often understand things without much explanation. They understand health concerns. They understand family priorities. They understand what it means to want time with children and grandchildren. And they understand the value of support when life gets difficult. And there can be real comfort in that. You don't always have to explain why certain things matter. Now people already understand. They often share ideas, encouragement, and practical ways of dealing with the kinds of challenges that come with this season in life. That can create a sense of support that is both practical and emotional. Maybe it's just someone checking up on you. Maybe it's someone explaining how something works in the community. Maybe it's just the quiet reassurance that you're surrounded by people who get it. For many retirees, that kind of understanding matters more than they expected. Now, let's talk about another quality that many people value highly in retirement. Peace of mind. Many Florida manufactured home communities offer a setting that feels a little more like an old-fashioned village. It feels more settled and often more peaceful than a typical neighborhood. In many communities, quiet hours are enforced which help reduce some of the disturbances that are common in more conventional neighborhoods. And when it comes to security, you also have choices. Some communities are gated. Some offer neighborhood watch programs. And in many of them, one of the first things you notice is that people are aware of one another and the whole environment feels calmer. And while no place is perfect and no one can promise absolute security, many people do say they feel more at ease in this kind of setting. There is comfort in a quiet community. Comfort in knowing people are nearby. Comfort in knowing that if something seems out of place, someone's likely to notice. And that sense of peace matters because one of the real gifts of retirement is supposed to be the ability to exhale a little, to stop living with constant tension, to feel settled. and the right community can contribute to that. Now, here's something I think really captures the appeal of this lifestyle. In a good community, peace and activity can exist together. It's not only about doing things, and it's not only about getting away from doing things. It's about having a setting where you can enjoy both. You can be active when you want to be. You can be quiet when you want to be. You can have people around you without feeling crowded. And you can enjoy the energy of the community without giving up your own space. That balance is very attractive because for many people, retirement is not about constant stimulation. It's about having a life that feels comfortably full. It's that Goldilocks balance between feeling overloaded with obligations and feeling underwhelmed with too little to do. On any given day, you might take a walk, stop by the pool, visit with someone, go to an event, or maybe do none of those things and simply enjoy the quiet, knowing that the active life is there whenever you want it. That's a very different experience from feeling cut off or having to create everything yourself. So when people ask what life feels like in a Florida manufactured home community, I think the best answer is this. It often feels easier. Easier to connect, easier to stay active, easier to meet people, easier to enjoy life. And that's really the point. The difference is not just the home. The difference is the rhythm of life around the home. A regular neighborhood may give you privacy, but it often leaves you more on your own to create your social life and daily rhythm. A good community gives you a setting where life can feel more connected, more flexible, and more enjoyable by design. That doesn't mean every community is right for every person, and it doesn't mean all communities feel the same, and it certainly doesn't mean this lifestyle is a one-sizefits-all. Finding the right community for you is still important. But it does help explain why so many people find this way of life so appealing once they understand what everyday life can feel like. And that brings us to the next important question. If life in these communities can feel this different, if some are active and peaceful and friendly and secure and supportive, then how do you tell which one is actually right for you? Because not every community has the same personality. So the next step is not just understanding this lifestyle. It's learning how to recognize the one that feels like home. And that's exactly what we'll explore next. If you'd like to keep moving forward, your next step is right here.

- What Are The Cultural Practices Of Phoenix Snowbirds?: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine [music] escaping winter's chill for a place where sunshine is a daily promise and the desert truly blooms. What kind of lifestyle awaits those who flock to Phoenix each year seeking warmth and community? The cultural practices of Phoenix snowbirds primarily revolve around a seasonal migration that creates a vibrant, active, and social lifestyle focused on enjoying the mild winter climate and abundant outdoor and community activities. These winter visitors temporarily call Phoenix home from roughly October to April. The core reason for these practices is the stark contrast in weather between their northern homes and the mild, sunny Arizona winter. This allows for a sustained period of outdoor recreation, socializing, and pursuing hobbies that might be impossible or uncomfortable elsewhere during colder months. Many snowbirds are retirees looking for an active lifestyle. And Phoenix provides a plethora of opportunities for golf, hiking, biking, tennis, and social gatherings. The sense of community is also a huge draw with many establishing long-term friendships with fellow snowbirds. This seasonal influx creates a distinct micro culture within the broader Phoenix community. Snowbirds typically establish a routine that maximizes their time in the sun. This often includes joining clubs or leagues for activities like pickle ball or bridge, volunteering at local charities, attending outdoor concerts and art festivals, and exploring the many state and national parks. Many also engage in community potlucks, neighborhood watch programs, and organized group excursions to nearby attractions such as Sedona or the Grand Canyon. They often shop at local farmers markets and frequent outdoor dining establishments. fully embracing the indooroutdoor living that Phoenix offers. A unique aspect is the prevalence of specialized snowbird communities which are often purpose-built with amenities like club houses, pools, and activity directors catering specifically to their needs and interests. These communities foster a strong sense of camaraderie among winter visitors and contribute significantly to the local economy during their stay. They are often keen to share their experiences, making new arrivals feel welcome and quickly integrated into the seasonal social fabric. This seasonal rhythm of Phoenix snowbirds enriches the local culture with a dynamic blend of active leisure, community engagement, and a shared appreciation for the desert's winter beauty. It offers a clear picture of how a community can thrive through shared seasonal experiences and a mutual desire for sunshine and companionship.

- When Do Snowbirds Leave Florida? - Southern Culture Collective: Kind: captions Language: en when do snowbirds leave Florida if you're curious about when the snowbirds pack up and head back North you're not alone let's break down the timeline to give you a clear idea of their departure snowbirds typically start arriving in Florida in the late fall or early winter but as the weather begins to warm up in their home regions they start to leave the exact months of their departure can vary but there's a general pattern most snowbirds begin leaving Florida in the spring months a common time frame for their departure is from late March to early may this period allows them to avoid the scorching summer heat that Florida is known for during the warmer months for some especially renters the departure can be as early as March 31st however many snowbirds tend to stay a bit longer often leaving after Easter or even as late as the end of April this is partly because the weather in their Northern homes starts to become more pleasant around this time making it easier for them to return owner snowbirds who often stay longer than renters might leave as late as May 30th this Extended Stay allows them to enjoy the milder Spring weather in Florida before heading back to their primary homes the roads and highways can get quite congested during this time especially around major routes like I75 as many snowbirds make their way back North so if you're planning to travel during this period it's a good idea to plan ahead and avoid the peak travel times in summary snowbirds generally leave Florida between late March and early May with some staying into April or even May depending on their individual preferences and circumstances

The US Is Out - Live Like a King for Less in These 10 Hotspots: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Welcome to a journey of discovery where we explore some of the most exciting alternative destinations for snowbirds. Imagine sundrrenched beaches, vibrant street life, delicious local cuisine, and smiling faces. Let's dive right in. For decades, the familiar southward migration led straight to the United States, Florida, Arizona. They were the promised lands of winter escape. But the world has opened up and a new wave of savvy travelers is realizing that the sun shines in many more exciting corners of the globe. Did you know that a whole quarter of new snowbirds are now setting their sights on international destinations? It's not just about escaping the cold anymore. It's about finding savings, immersing in vibrant cultures, and embracing a life that feels a little more adventurous. Today we're embarking on a whirlwind tour of the top 10 alternative destinations where Canadian snowbirds can trade their parkas for paradise, their heating bills for delicious street food, and the mundane for the magnificent. Get ready to have your travel horizons broadened because your next winter escape might be more global and more incredible than you ever imagined. If the idea of swapping snow for sunshine and saving money while you're at it excites you, smash that like button and make sure you subscribe because this is where smart travelers like you come to discover their next great adventure. Let's be real for a moment. The US has its charms. It's familiar. The language is mostly the same. And for many years, it felt like a natural choice. But the landscape is changing and frankly your wallet might thank you for looking elsewhere. The US dollar remains strong, making everything from groceries to entertainment more expensive for Canadians. Rental prices in popular snowbird states have skyrocketed, leaving many feeling squeezed. And let's not even delve into the complexities and potential astronomical costs of healthcare if you're not adequately insured. According to recent data from Snowbird Adviser, a significant shift is underway. One in four snowbirds is now actively considering a winter home outside the United States. Why? The reasons are compelling. In nations like Mexico, Portugal, and Thailand, your Canadian dollar stretches remarkably further. You can often find better quality food at lower prices. And perhaps most importantly, you can bask in equally warm or even warmer winters without breaking the bank. So, are you ready to ditch the predictable and discover a winter escape that offers more bang for your buck, more cultural richness, and a whole lot more excitement? Let's dive into five incredible destinations that might just become your new snowbird paradise. Ah, Mexico. The land of eternal sunshine, the birthplace of tequila, and a country whose vibrant culture and incredibly affordable cost of living are no longer a secret to savvy Canadian snowbirds. It's geographically close, offers a comfortable level of familiarity, and boasts a thriving community of Canadians who have already discovered its many charms. Consider these key spots for your Mexican winter escape. Porto Viarta, a perennial favorite, PV offers stunning beaches, a lively nightlife, and a wellestablished and welcoming expat community. You'll find everything you need and plenty of fellow Canadians to connect with. Lake Chapala. Looking for a more tranquil experience with mild, spring-like weather? The charming colonial towns around Lake Chapala offer a relaxed pace of life and incredibly affordable rent, often under 1,000 CAD a month. Mayan Riviera. If crystal clear turquoise waters and white sand beaches are calling your name, the Mayan Riviera delivers. You can find beautiful beachside condos that won't cost you a fortune compared to similar properties in the US. So, why does Mexico work so well for Canadian snowbirds? Flights short and cheap. Getting to Mexico from most major Canadian cities is quick and often surprisingly affordable. Language: No problem. While learning some Spanish is always appreciated, English is widely spoken in tourist areas and expat communities. cost of living, luxury on a budget. From delicious and inexpensive food to affordable housing and entertainment, your Canadian dollar will go a long way in Mexico. However, a word of caution. While healthcare in Mexico is generally affordable, it's crucial to plan ahead. Travel insurance is an absolute must. And if you venture beyond the main tourist hubs, brushing up on your Spanish will greatly enhance your experience. Dreaming of a true paradise? Look no further than Costa Rica. This Central American gem is renowned for its breathtaking natural beauty, its commitment to ecoourism, and a welcoming atmosphere that draws in expats from around the globe. Here are a couple of key areas to consider. Tamarindo. If you're a surfer at heart or simply love the energy of beach life, Tamarindo on the Pacific coast is your haven. It boasts consistent waves, a warm and friendly community, and a vibrant social scene. Central Valley. Not a fan of intense tropical heat? The Central Valley is where many smart expats head. Its higher elevation provides a cooler, fresher climate and a more relaxed, traditional pace of life. What makes Costa Rica a compelling choice? It boasts a stable economy, making it a relatively secure place to live. There's a huge and well-established expat community, providing a built-in support network. Believe it or not, a comfortable lifestyle can be achieved on around $50 Canadian a day, depending on your preferences. Keep in mind, however, that popular rentals in Costa Rica tend to get snapped up quickly, so it's essential to start your search early. And while not excessively expensive, flights to Costa Rica are generally longer than those to Mexico, so booking in advance is highly recommended. Next on our list is Panama. This vibrant Central American nation offers a unique blend of modernity and natural beauty. And here's a significant bonus. Unlike Costa Rica, Panama uses the US dollar as its currency, eliminating the hassle and uncertainty of fluctuating exchange rates. Here's why Panama is gaining popularity among Canadian snowbirds. It offers a combination of tropical warmth, attractive tax perks for retirees, and expat friendly cities with modern amenities. The mountain town of Bokeete is a particular draw, boasting stunning scenery and a large and welcoming community of Canadians and other expats. You'll feel right at home. Many report being able to live comfortably, even finding a dream retirement spot for around $1,500 Canadian a month. Now, let's journey across the Atlantic to a land of captivating history, stunning scenery, and surprisingly affordable living Portugal. Imagine waking up to the gentle sound of the ocean breeze, stepping outside to a mild 15° C day in January instead of snow drifts and spending your afternoons exploring charming cobblestone streets. This could be your reality in Portugal. Here's why Portugal is attracting increasing attention from Canadian snowbirds. It boasts mild winters, making it a pleasant escape from the harsh Canadian cold. Affordable rental properties can be found in many parts of the country. You can embrace a relaxed and enriching European lifestyle without a hefty price tag. Believe it or not, finding rent for around $800 Canadian dollars a month is very achievable in certain areas and the language barrier. In many expat heavy areas, English is commonly spoken. However, there are a couple of considerations to keep in mind. Flights to Portugal are significantly longer than those to North or Central America. Additionally, visa rules for stays longer than 90 days can be tricky, so careful planning is essential if you're considering an extended winter sojourn. And now for something completely different, our wild card pick, the destination that offers an incredible blend of exotic culture and unbelievable affordability, Thailand. If you're looking for a truly immersive experience that won't break the bank, Thailand might just be your perfect winter escape. Here's why Thailand is captivating adventurous snowbirds. You can realistically live comfortably, including rent, food, and fun for around $1,000 Canadian dollars a month, or even less in some areas. The beaches are simply unreal, offering breathtaking beauty and a chance to truly unwind. And the food, life-changing. From flavorful street food to exquisite restaurant dining, your taste buds are in for a treat. However, be prepared for a long flight from Canada. Additionally, visa extensions for longer stays require careful planning and application. But for those willing to make the journey, the rewards of a winter in Thailand can be truly extraordinary. Looking for a Caribbean escape without the hefty price tag often associated with the region? Say hello to the Dominican Republic. This island nation offers a fantastic alternative to Florida with gorgeous beaches and a welcoming culture. Why it works for snowbirds. Enjoy the convenience of direct flights from many Canadian cities, making travel relatively easy. Indulge in the breathtaking gorgeous beaches and connect with the friendly locals. Find surprisingly affordable rent with options as low as $800 Canadian per month in some areas. Things to consider. Be mindful that electricity costs can be high, so factor this into your budget. It's wise to research and stick to expat friendly zones known for their safety and amenities. Ready for another dose of exotic adventure on a budget? Let's head to Vietnam. This Southeast Asian nation offers a captivating blend of rich history, incredible natural beauty, and a surprisingly low cost of living. Why Vietnam could be your next winter haven. Prepare to be amazed. You can often find luxury living for around $800 Canadian a month. Immerse yourself in the incredible food, connect with the friendly people, and explore the amazing culture. Enjoy warm weather, discover beautiful beaches, and explore breathtaking mountains. Vietnam has it all. Key considerations: Visas require planning, but the process is generally manageable with some preparation. Similar to Thailand, flights are long, but many find the unique experience well worth the journey. Looking for a Caribbean escape where you don't even need to worry about learning a new language? Look no further than Bise. This small Central American nation boasts stunning natural beauty and English as its official language. Why Bleise might be your perfect snowbird destination. Say goodbye to language barriers. English is the official language, making it easy to navigate and connect with locals. Discover gorgeous beaches, explore incredible coral reefs, and immerse yourself in the vibrant marine life. Enjoy a lower cost of living compared to both the US and Canada, allowing your retirement dollars to stretch further. Things to keep in mind, big city amenities are limited compared to larger destinations, offering a more laid-back lifestyle. Bise is a small country, so it's advisable to plan your travels and accommodations in advance. For a taste of the Dutch Caribbean, consider Curisowl. This island offers a unique blend of European charm and tropical beauty. Why Curasau could be your winter escape? Discover one of the most affordable places on our list, allowing your budget to stretch further than you might imagine. - What Is Snowbird Tourism? - Everything About Phoenix: Kind: captions Language: en What is snowbird tourism? Have you ever wondered why so many people flock to Phoenix during the winter months? This phenomenon is known as snowbird tourism. It involves seasonal migration, where individuals, often from colder northern parts of North America, travel to warmer southern areas to escape the harsh winter. Phoenix, Arizona is a prime destination for these travelers seeking sunshine and milder temperatures. Each year, thousands of snowbirds make their way to Phoenix, significantly impacting the local economy. These visitors typically stay for several months, which can lead to a noticeable increase in the city's population during winter. This influx supports local businesses from restaurants to shops and recreational facilities, providing a boost to the economy. One of the appealing aspects of snowbird tourism in Phoenix is the wide range of activities available. Snowbirds can enjoy outdoor adventures like hiking and biking, cultural events, and worldclass golf courses. The city offers various accommodations including vacation home rentals and recreational vehicle parks catering to different preferences and budgets. However, the large number of snowbirds does come with challenges. The increased population can strain local resources such as transportation and housing. Additionally, the rise in vehicle usage during the winter months can lead to air pollution, which is a concern for both residents and visitors alike. Despite these challenges, Phoenix continues to embrace snowbirds as part of its vibrant winter community. The city benefits from the economic boost and the cultural exchange that comes with hosting these seasonal visitors. Local officials and community leaders work to balance the needs of both residents and snowbirds, ensuring that Phoenix remains an attractive destination for those seeking a warm winter retreat. In summary, snowbird tourism plays a vital role in Phoenix's history and cultural heritage travel. It reflects the city's growth and the rise of tourism, showcasing how seasonal visitors contribute to the local economy while enjoying all that Phoenix has to offer.

today only ruins and wreckage remind us of a time in which the sea ran red with the blood of thousands of whales in the early 20th century margarine soap and nitroglycerine for weapons were produced from whale oil the discovery of a vast whale population led to a true gold rush grease from whale blubber was the basis of the industrial revolution and became very expensive whalers lived and worked in six stations here processing 175 000 whales in 60 years they erected buildings on penguin breeding grounds bringing with them domestic animals and rats the workers made omelettes from penguin eggs and showed little respect for nature only in 1965 did the whalers leave south georgia for good and the animals could once again reign supreme on the island millions of antarctic fur seals were doomed - More Canadian snowbirds decide wintering in the U.S. over economic tensions: Kind: captions Language: en It seems more Canadian snowbirds are deciding to fly elsewhere this winter to avoid the US as their vacation destination. >> number of Canadians crossing the border has been steadily dropping and a new survey finds the trend is likely to continue well into next year. Here's Pat Foran and Consumer Alert, Pat. Well, thanks Nathan and Michelle. Next month many retired Canadians would normally be packing their suitcases to head to Florida, Arizona and other sunny US destinations, but a new survey shows a massive drop in snowbirds planning to cross the border choosing other hotspots instead. More Canadians are turning their backs on travel to the US deciding to either stay home or choose sunny destinations somewhere else. A survey by the Travel Health Insurance Association of Canada found that only 26% are likely to go to the US. That's a 37% drop from last year. The survey found of those 61 years of age and older only 10% are likely to go to the US. That's a 66% drop from the year before. We've certainly seen a move by some to get away from maybe ownership or long-term rentals in the US in favor of other southern climates. The survey found Canadians are concerned about rising travel costs, political tensions, exchange rates and safety. The shift comes at a time when the US is introducing new entry requirements which mean Canadians staying for longer than 30 days need to register and provide their fingerprints, be photographed and pay a $30 fee. Having your photo taken and your your fingerprints done feels really weird and wrong to us, but the reality is is many countries all over the world have already been doing this for the last decade. Travel expert Barry Choi says while travel to the US is down, he believes many families will continue to go to US attractions, but keep a lower profile and not post on social media. He says snowbirds who don't own property in the US will also find it hard to stay away. If someone's a snowbird and they own a vacation home in in Florida or Arizona, of course they're going to go. Canadians avoiding the states are also planning to spend winter months in Europe including Portugal and Spain. Trips to Mexico and the Caribbean will also depend on the weather and the cost of travel. If American destinations cut prices, it may be enough for some to reconsider a US trip. If some of the US destinations start to go on sale, maybe that easy trip over the border still prevails. And wherever you go, if you leave Canada, you're advised to get travel insurance in case of a medical emergency or unexpected health issue. When you're out of the country, both are not covered under Canadian health plans. On your side, I'm Pat Foran. If you have a consumer story idea, email us at alert@ctv.ca.

But since mid January, she has noticed it's gone from a buyer market to a sellers market. And she notes there are three reasons for this shift in the market. The US Canadian dollar exchange rate, the new condo assessments, and the cost of insuring your property. So most of the snowbirds that Spino is working with are looking to sell their condos, which are second home for her clients. She says some families just can't keep up with the rising cost of HOAs, which have increased to keep up with the rising cost to ensure condo buildings. So, they are taking a cut on their investment just to sell it off. If they don't even have the money to pay that 20 $25,000 assessment, they put it for sale on the market and in the contract, the buyer is going to be liable for this assessment and they have to buy it to pay for it like at closing. So then they're selling it for a bit less the seller. Spino tells CBS12 News the exchange rate is also a big factor for Canadians who want their money to go a little further. So, as of right now, I just checked this morning, one US dollar is equivalent to.70 cents in Canada. And even if they go the renting route during high season here in Florida, it is very expensive. - Canadian Snowbirds New US Border Rule You Must Know - 2025 Edition: Kind: captions Language: en Hey folks, if you're a Canadian planning to spend your winter down in Florida, Arizona, or any sunny US spot, listen up because the border rules just got weird and confusing. I'm talking about what used to be a carefree drive south for our snowbird community turning into something that can feel like you're walking into a checkpoint. Today, we're unpacking what it really means when you cross from Canada into the US for longer than 30 days. what forms you need, what you don't, and how to avoid a major surprise at the border. So, here's the story. Canadians have long enjoyed heading down to the US for the winter. RVs, motor homes, beach houses, you name it. But starting April 11th, 2025, a rule kicked in. If you're entering the US and planning to stay 30 days or more, and you didn't get an electronic I94 record, you now must register with US authorities. Now, many Canadians entering the US by air already get that electronic I94. Problem is, if you cross by land, especially you snowbirds pulling a motor home south, you may not have had that issued. So, you might unknowingly be subject to this additional registration. And there's the confusion. Some travelers at the land border are being fingerprinted or photographed or getting charged dollars for registration fees. Even though Canadians shouldn't always be fingerprinted, some others breeze through. The difference entry method plus what the border officer decides in that moment. Imagine arriving at a US land crossing expecting the usual wave through and instead being pulled aside for secondary inspection. That's exactly what happened to some snowbirds this year. The stakes are real. Non-compliance can mean fines up to USD $5,000 or even jail time. Yes, that. So, here's where we break down what you need to do to stay safe and traveler happy. One, check whether you received an electronic I94 as soon as you arrive or even before. Go to the official CBP website, i94.cbp.jets.gov, and select get most recent I94 and enter your information. If you see a record for your latest entry, you're likely good. You've already registered. Two, if you did not receive an I94 and you're staying 30 days or more, you must register via USCIS. This means creating an online account, filling out form G325R, biographic information, and carrying the proof of registration. For Canadians, this process usually does not include fingerprinting. Three, at the border crossing, especially land crossings, be proactive. If you know you're staying long, ask for an I94 at the point of entry. If the officer issues one, great. If they don't, know you're going to need the registration. Some ports allow a provisional I94 application up to 7 days before crossing. Fee applicable, but approval isn't guaranteed. Four, carry your evidence. Whether an I94 or the USCIS registration confirmation have it printed or accessible. Border officers may ask to see it. Some guidance says a mobile version may not be accepted. Five. Stay under 30 days means you are generally safe from the registration rule. If you're planning a shorter stay, many of these extra steps may not apply. But if you cross with the intention of staying winter long, you'll want to double check. Let me share a quick real world example. A couple driving their RV from Calgary arrived at the Sweetg Grass Montana border. They didn't realize they needed to check for an I94. They were treated like they hadn't registered, fingerprinted, photographed, charged $60. Another couple crossed at PeaceBridge, Buffalo for only 30 days, and nobody mentioned registration. It shows how inconsistent things are, which is why you need to go in with eyes open. All right, here's what I want you to do right now. Before you head south this winter, check your I94 status online. If you plan to stay 30 days or more and don't see the I94, register via USCIS ASAP. Print or save your registration or I94 information. At the border, be confident. Know what you need. If questions come, answer clearly, calmly, and carry the proof you're compliant. And if you're unsure, don't just wing it. Consider reaching out to an immigration professional. Better safe than stuck in secondary for hours. If you found this helpful, hit the like button. Subscribe for more travel and immigration updates, especially for Canadians heading to the US. Leave a comment. Where are you planning to go this winter, and how do you feel about these new rules? I'll drop a link in the description below with the official I94 check site and some trusted resources. Thanks for watching.

- The Untold Truth of Snowbird Brown… What REALLY Happened After The Show?: Kind: captions Language: en You thought you knew Snowbird Brown, but what if I told you she almost lost everything? Her family, her health, and the life she once knew. This is the story no one is talking about. You probably remember her as the quiet, wild-hearted girl from the wilderness. But what really happened to Snowbird Brown after the cameras stopped rolling? Because behind that calm personality, there's a story filled with pain, survival, and a life that completely changed. Snowbird Brown, also known as Birdie, became famous through the hit reality show Alaskan Bush People. A show that followed the Brown family living deep in the wilderness, far away from modern society. But here's what many people don't realize. That life wasn't as simple as it looked. From a very young age, Snowbird was different from the rest of her family. While others were loud and adventurous, she was quiet, deeply connected to nature, and incredibly emotional. She didn't need attention. She didn't chase fame. She just wanted peace. And maybe that's why fans connected with her the most. But everything changed. In 2021, tragedy hit the Brown family hard. Their father, Billy Brown, the man who built their entire lifestyle, suddenly passed away after a seizure. And Snowbird was there. She witnessed those final moments and that moment broke something inside her. Losing not just a father but the leader of the family. After his death, everything started falling apart. The family dynamic changed. Responsibilities shifted and Snowbird had to grow stronger whether she was ready or not. But then something even more terrifying happened. In 2022, Snowbird began experiencing severe abdominal pain. At first, she ignored it just like someone used to surviving harsh conditions would. But the pain got worse. So bad that she couldn't even stand properly. Eventually, she was rushed to the hospital. And that's when doctors discovered something shocking. A large tumor inside her body. At that moment, everything changed again. There was fear, uncertainty, and the possibility that her life could take a completely different turn. She had to undergo surgery. And while the operation was successful, the emotional impact stayed with her. Imagine going from living freely in the wild to suddenly fighting for your own health. That kind of shift changes a person. But here's where Snowbird surprised everyone. Instead of breaking down, she started rebuilding herself. After the show slowed down and eventually stopped, Snowbird chose a different path. She didn't chase Hollywood. She didn't try to become a typical celebrity. Instead, she went back to what she loved the most, nature, animals, and a simple life. She even started creating handmade items like dreamcatchers and crafts, turning her passion into something meaningful. And slowly she began building her own identity. Not as just a member of the Brown family, but as Snowbird, her own person. At the same time, the Brown family itself was facing major struggles. After Billy's death, tensions started rising. There were rumors, conflicts, and even legal issues involving some family members. The once tight-knit family was no longer the same. And through all of this, Snowbird stayed mostly quiet. No drama, no public fights, just distance. And maybe that silence says everything. Because sometimes the strongest people are the ones who choose peace over chaos. Today, Snowbird continues to live a low-key life. She spends time with animals, stays connected to nature, and occasionally shares glimpses of her life online. But one question still remains. Is she truly happy? Or is she still carrying the weight of everything she went through? Because when you look deeper, her story isn't just about survival in the wilderness. It's about surviving loss, fear, and change. And maybe that's why people are still so drawn to her. Not because she's loud, not because she's dramatic, but because she's real. And in a world full of noise, that's rare. So the next time you see Snowbird Brown, remember you're not just looking at a reality TV star. You're looking at someone who faced loss, fear, and uncertainty and still chose to keep going.

- History Book Review: Florida's Snowbirds: Spectacle, Mobility, and Community Since 1945 by Godefr...: Kind: captions Language: en This is the summary of Florida's snowbirds, spectacle, mobility, and community since 1945 by God Freudis Rossia's Lorzen. Every year when the temperature drops below freezing, countless people do, as the thermometers do, head south. Since the end of the Second World War, Florida has been one of the most desired vacation and retirement destinations for generations of snowbirds. Florida's snowbirds examines the effects of the seasonal migration not only for travelers but also for local Floridaidians developing numerous themes including leisure state promoted tourism citizenship and business investment god Rossia's laws and considers advertisements movies policy makers and the behavior of snowbirds in Florida to provide the most thorough study of the vacation state to date also looks at the temporary communities of Canadians, Cubequa, New Englanders, and Midwesterners that develop, showing how they blur the lines that usually divide national and regional identities and youth and age. An insightful work full of amusing details. Florida's snowbirds pieces together a complete cultural atlas of Florida snowbirds that goes far beyond the familiar postcards they send home. For more history books and book reviews, visit www.historybookmix.com. Historybookmix.com
