- The Financial Truth About Snowbirding Nobody Tells You: Kind: captions Language: en Most people think snowbirds are wealthy retirees with vacation homes and money to burn. I used to think that, too. But here's what nobody talks about. Some of the most financially savvy people I have ever met are snowbirds, teachers, nurses, and small business owners. Regular people on fixed incomes who figured out a system that lets them live better and keep more of their money. One woman I met in Florida last winter, she was paying less for her 4 month stay in the sun than she would have spent sitting home in Ohio. Heating bills, snow removal, and higher grocery prices in winter. It all adds up, and she finally did the math. Here's the thing about the snowbird lifestyle that nobody on the internet is talking about. Done right, it is not a luxury. It is a financial decision. And in this video, I'm going to break down exactly how the numbers work. Because once you see it, you cannot unsee it. I'm Sandy. Welcome to Snowbird Sandy, where we talk about money, retirement, and living your absolute best life in your 50s and 60s. I think you're going to want to stick around for this one. But before we get into the numbers, let me tell you a little bit about who I am and why I started this channel. I spent most of my working life doing what I was told to do. save money, max out the 401k, pay off the house, wait for retirement. And I did all of that. And then retirement actually arrived. And I realized that nobody had ever taught me what to do with it, how to make my money last, how to actually enjoy my life without constantly worrying about running out. That is what this channel is about. Smart, practical, realworld money decisions for people in their 50s and 60s. Not complicated Wall Street strategies, not get-richqu nonsense, just honest conversations about how to make the most of the money you worked so hard to save. And snowbirding, it turned out to be one of the best financial experiments I ever ran on myself. So, if you are thinking about retirement, already in retirement, or just trying to figure out how to stretch your dollars further, you are in the right place. Hit that subscribe button because we are going to cover a lot of ground on this channel. Let's start with something that most people have never actually calculated. The real cost of a northern winter. I'm not talking about being uncomfortable. I'm talking about cold, hard dollars leaving your account every single month from November through March. Think about it. Heating bills. Here in the Midwest and Northeast, average heating costs run between $300 and $600 a month in peak winter. That is $1,500 to $3,000 just in heat over a 5mon winter. Then add snow removal. Whether you're paying a service or paying a neighbor's kid, that's another $100 to $200 a month. Winter car maintenance, salt damage, battery replacements, and tire wear. Studies show winter driving adds hundreds of dollars a year to vehicle costs. And here's one nobody talks about. Health care. Cold weather is genuinely harder on your body. Joint pain flares and respiratory issues worsen. Seasonal depression is real and it has real costs in doctor visits, medications, and lost productivity. When I added all of this up for my own situation, I was spending somewhere between $800 and $1,200 a month just on the extra costs that winter created every single year for decades. And I just accepted it as normal. Once I saw that number, that's when I started asking, "What if I just left?" Okay, here is the first tip, and this is the one I wish somebody had handed me before I ever booked a single night away. Before you decide whether snowboarding makes financial sense for you, you need to run your personal break even number. And it is simpler than it sounds. Here is the formula. Take everything winter costs you at home. Your heating, snow removal, extra health care, and any other winter specific expenses. Add them up for the months you'd be gone. That is your winter cost number. Now add up what snowboarding would cost you. your rental down south, transportation to get there, and any extra living expenses. That is your snowbird cost number. Subtract one from the other. That gap is what snowbirding either costs you or saves you. And here's what surprises most people. That gap is almost always much smaller than they expected. Sometimes it is zero. Sometimes snowboarding actually wins. But here's where it gets really interesting, and this is the part most people miss entirely. If you rent your home while you are gone, even partially, that rental income goes directly into that equation. And for a lot of people, it flips the math completely in their favor. I have talked to snowbirds who are netting positive after every single expense, meaning their southern lifestyle is literally paying for itself and then some. Tip number two is all about timing, and it is one of the most straightforward ways to cut your snowbird costs by 20 to 35% without changing anything else about your plans. Here is how seasonal rental pricing actually works. Snowbird destinations have three pricing tiers, and most people only ever see one of them. The first tier is peak season pricing. This is what you get if you start looking in October or November for a January departure. The good properties are mostly gone. What is left is overpriced and undized. This is where people overpay and then say snowbirding is too expensive. The second tier is early bird pricing. Property owners in Florida, Arizona, and Texas know that their best, most reliable tenants, people staying 2 to four months, book early. If you reach out in June or July for the coming winter, you will often find the same property at 20 to 30% less, sometimes more. The third tier, and this is the hidden gem, is shoulder season pricing. The first two weeks of November and the last two weeks of March are dramatically cheaper than peak months. If your schedule has any flexibility at all, shifting your window by even 2 to 3 weeks can save you hundreds of dollars a month. And here is a negotiating tip that works more often than people realize. Offer to pay for the full season upfront. Many private landlords and small property management companies will knock an additional 5 to 10% off for a guaranteed no hassle tenant who pays in full. It is worth asking. One more thing, look beyond Airbnb. Snowbird specific platforms like snowbird rentals.com, RV park directories, and local Facebook groups for snowbird communities often have listings that never appear on the big platforms and they are almost always cheaper. Okay, this is the big one and I want you to pay close attention here because this single tip done correctly can save you thousands of dollars every single year in retirement. It is called establishing tax residency in your snowbird state. And most snowbirds either do not know about it or they think it is too complicated to bother with. It is not. Here is the basic idea. Seven states in the US have zero state income tax. Florida, Texas, Nevada, Wyoming, South Dakota, Washington, and Alaska. Meanwhile, states like California, New York, Minnesota, and Illinois have some of the highest state income tax rates in the country, some as high as 13%. For someone pulling $80,000 a year from a pension, 401k, or social security in retirement, the difference between paying New York state taxes and paying Florida state taxes is roughly 6 to 8,000 a year, every year. That is not a one-time savings. that compounds over a retirement. Now, here is the rule you need to know. Most states use what is called the 183day rule to determine tax residency. Spend more than 183 days, that is 6 months plus 1 day, in a state, and that state considers you a resident for tax purposes. Spend fewer than 183 days in your high tax home state. And if you have established doicile somewhere else, they generally cannot tax your income. And there is a bonus on top of that. Many snowbird friendly states also do not tax social security income. Florida, Texas, and Nevada do not touch your social security check. Some northern states take up to 85% of your benefit and tax it. Changing your residency could save an additional $1 to $2,500 a year just on that income stream alone. Now, I have to be very clear here. Changing your tax doicile is not as simple as just spending winters in Florida. You need to get a Florida driver's license, register your car there, register to vote there, update your legal documents, and you need to genuinely sever ties with your old state because states like New York and California audit snowbirds specifically. They will look at where your doctor is, where your safe deposit box is, where you spend the holidays. This is not something to do casually, but it is absolutely something worth exploring with a CPA who specializes in multi-state taxation because the numbers are real. And for many people in retirement, this is the single highest return financial move available to them. Now, let's talk about something that does not get enough attention. What happens to your home back north while you are gone? This is an area where I made a costly mistake in my first year, and I do not want you to repeat it. First, insurance. Most standard homeowners policies have a vacancy clause. If your home sits unoccupied for more than 30 to 60 days, your coverage can be reduced or even voided if something goes wrong. a burst pipe, a break-in, a roof leak. Your claim could be denied simply because nobody was home. Before you leave, call your insurance company. Tell them exactly how long you will be gone. Ask about a vacancy rider or a snowbird endorsement. It usually costs very little and it means you are actually covered. Second, get a house checker. This is someone, a neighbor, a family member, a paid service who physically walks through your home every week or two while you are away. They check for water leaks, heating issues, and anything unusual. This is not optional. It is a $50 to $100 a month expense that could save you tens of thousands. Third, if you are renting your home while you are gone, make sure you understand landlord tenant law in your state. Screen your tenants carefully. Use a written lease even for short-term rentals and make sure your rental income is documented properly for your taxes because as we discussed there are real deductions available to you. I have spent most of this video talking about numbers and that is intentional because I want you to take snowbirding seriously as a financial strategy, not just a nice idea. But I want to take just a minute to talk about something that does not show up on a spreadsheet and yet has a very real financial value. Your health. The research on this is consistent. Warmer winter climates reduce the physical stress of cold weather living. People with arthritis, respiratory conditions, and cardiovascular issues report measurably better health outcomes when they winter in warmer climates. Fewer flare-ups, fewer doctor visits, lower medication needs, and the mental health piece is just as significant. Seasonal depression is real. The isolation of a long northern winter is real. The loss of motivation and activity is real. And all of those things have financial consequences in health care costs, in reduced productivity, in quality of life. The snowbird community I found in my first year was one of the most genuinely joyful groups of people I have ever been around. Active, engaged, social people who had decided that retirement was not a waiting room. It was a destination. And here is the financial truth about that. People who are healthier and happier in retirement spend less money on health care and spend more years enjoying the money they saved. That is not a soft benefit. That is a hard financial return. So, let's bring this all home. Snowbirding is not just for the wealthy. It is not just for full retirees. And it is definitely not just about escaping the cold, though I will never complain about that part. Done strategically, snowbirding is a financial tool. It can reduce your cost of living, lower your tax burden, protect your health, and give you a quality of life in retirement that most people only dream about. The three tips I want you to walk away with today. Number one, run your personal break even number before you write off snowbirding as too expensive. The math may surprise you. Number two, if you are even thinking about going snowbird next winter, start looking at rentals this summer. Early booking is one of the highest return, lowest effort money moves in this whole lifestyle. And number three, have a conversation with a CPA about your tax residency situation. For many people in retirement, this is the single most valuable financial conversation they can have. Coming up on this channel, I am going to do a full cost breakdown of my first snowbird season. Every dollar, every mistake, every surprise. I am also going to do a deep dive video on the tax residency question because it deserves its own full episode. If you do not want to miss those, subscribe and hit the bell right now. And drop a comment below and tell me where are you from and where would you most want to spend your first snowbird winter. I read every single one and I love hearing from you. I'm Sandy. This is snowbird Sandy and I will see you in the sunshine.

- Canadian snowbirds and taxes - Wealth and tax planning with Michelle Connolly | Wellington-Altus: Kind: captions Language: en-CA Can a Canadian citizen and resident with no other U.S. tax ties be subject to the various U.S. income tax regimes? Simply, yes. The two most common factors that may expose Canadians to U.S. taxes are time spent in the US and ownership of U.S. situs assets. Let's take a look at time spent. Canadians, including snowbirds, may be considered U.S. resident aliens if they spend too much time in the U.S. requiring them to file U.S. income tax and information returns. There are two 183 day tests to consider. The first, which is very common and most are aware of, is spending 183 days or more during a given calendar year. The second is a substantial presence test. If a Canadian spends 31 days or more in a calendar year and 183 weighted days or more during a three year period, which includes the current and immediately preceding two years, and collectively it's over 183 days. They've demonstrated substantial presence. Spend less than 122 days per year or four months to avoid exceeding the 183 weighted day test. Keep in mind also what counts as a day. It's any portion of any one day that counts. For those Canadians who have exceeded the first test, they may be able to claim an exemption under the Canada-U.S. tax treaty by filing Form 8833, which is a Treaty Based Return Position Disclosure. All applicable U.S. income and information returns still have to be submitted. You may not have to owe the tax, but you still have to file all the U.S. returns. For those who have exceeded 183 weighted days by filing Form 8840, the Closer Connection Exception Statement for Aliens. A Canadian acknowledges the substantial time that they've spent in the U.S., but attest that they have stronger residential ties to Canada than the U.S.. Stronger Canadian ties would include, but are not limited to, principal residence, personal belongings, bank investment account, wealth ties, earned income, established memberships. The second aspect is if Canadians own U.S. So what are U.S. situs assets? Real property, tangible personal property, U.S. securities, those that are traded on U.S. stock exchanges or ownership in U.S. private corporations. Cash accounts with U.S. brokerage firms located in situ in the U.S.. Certain U.S. debt obligations. U.S. mutual funds, including money market funds. No, these are U.S. situs funds are not Canadian funds that are traded in U.S. dollars. In 2023, if a Canadian has worldwide wealth in excess of 12.92 million U.S. dollars, and owns a dollar of U.S. situs assets, they'll be subject to U.S. estate tax. Worldwide wealth includes all real estate, business interests, financial accounts, insurance ownership, etc. Take note that the unified credit changes annually and it's anticipated to drop substantially in the next three years, meaning more Canadians may be subject to U.S. estate tax based on owning U.S. We at Wellington-Altus ask questions particularly when it comes to exposure to the U.S. tax regimes and have recommendations to minimize such given a client's particular wants and interests. Hope these points resonate with you and remember, you don't have to be the expert here. If you have questions, please reach out to your Wellington-Altus Advisor.

- What Tax Am I Really Paying On My US Assets? Canadian Snowbirds, be cautious: Kind: captions Language: en Canadian snowbirds and US taxes. Staying out of trouble. Hey snowbirds, thinking of trading your shovel for a golf club this winter? Before you head south, let's talk about something that could land you in hot water. US taxes. Welcome to the channel. My name is Anthony Jmoni. I'm a senior wealth advisor with Ages Wealth Management, a part of Wellington Altus Private Wealth. The goal of our channel is to educate Canadians on the complexities of personal finance and to provide clarity to help them reach their goals. If you're new to the channel, please make sure to subscribe and click the like button. Canadian snowbirds and US taxes. What you need to know. Just because you're Canadian doesn't mean the IRS won't come knocking if you spend too much time in the States. Yep. Even without a green card or US citizenship, you could be seen as a US resident for tax purposes. That means filing returns with the IRS and potentially paying US tax on your worldwide income. So, how do they decide if you're a resident? There are two main tests. Number one, the calendar year test. If you're in the US, 183 days or more in one calendar year, you're a US tax resident. Number two, the substantial presence test. This one's trickier. It looks at how many days you've spent in the US over the last 3 years. Here's the math. All the days this year plus onethird of the days last year plus 16th of the days 2 years ago. If that total hits 183 days or more, congrats. You're a US tax resident whether you meant to be or not. Take Marco and Louise. They retired in 2018, bought a condo in Arizona, and spend every winter there from November to April. In 20121, even though they were only in the US for 151 days, their total weighted days over 3 years hit over 226. Boom. They triggered the substantial presence test. But don't worry, there are ways to stay out of IRS territory. Option one, the closer connection exemption. If you were in the US fewer than 183 days this year, but still triggered the three-year test, you can file IRS form 8840. This tells the IRS, hey, I may visit often, but Canada is still my home. You'll need to prove your ties. Things like where you live, where your family is, your car registration, voting status, and where you earn your income. Option two, the tax treaty exemption. If you were in the US more than 183 days in a single year, you'll have to file a full US tax return, but you might still be able to dodge double taxation using the Canadian US tax treating filing form 8833 to claim you're still more connected to Canada. Here are some quick tips to avoid US tax headaches. Stay under 122 days per year. That's the magic number to fly under the IRS radar. Track your days carefully. Even a 1-hour grocery trip counts. Exceptions exist, like medical stopovers, but don't count on them. Remember, Canada and the US share immigration info. The US knows how long you've been there. So, snowbirds, plan smart, stay warm, and don't let taxes ruin your winter escape. For more tailored advice, chat with a crossborder tax specialist or your Wellington Altus advisor. Subscribe for more tax tips and financial advice. Thanks for watching. Safe travels and sunny days ahead.

Should you Florida snowbird or should you just go full-on full-time Florida resident? And those two paths sound similar in a lot of ways, but in actuality, I think they veer quite a bit. They come with different financial outcomes. There's risk when it comes to tax, healthcare realities, just very different lifestyle trade-offs overall. And let me just pause for one quick second before I go further to define kind of a clarity perspective for this video. A Florida snowbird to me is someone who lives in the state of Florida seasonally but keeps their primary residence. They keep legal ties and basically their entire core identity um in another state. In another way of saying it, a snowbird comes to Florida for part of the year, usually the winter time, uh but does not fully relocate their entire life. They typically keep the main house somewhere else, often their driver's license as well back home, uh voter registration, the doctor's residency when it comes to tax in the original state. It's is it doing that or is it just changing where you pay that money? Snowbird life often feels financially safer just because it's purely flexible. You don't have to fully commit. You can rent for the season. You can uh test different cities. If you don't like anything, you just pick up a leap. That flexibility actually I do personally value. I I'm a little transient. And then on top of that, if you're renting, uh, to say that Southwest Florida rentals are cheap, I wouldn't I wouldn't suggest is true, especially during the winter peak pricing because what you're doing basically is you're coming to someone else's house they own. You're asking to borrow it for a portion of time during the most unattractive single periods that it is to live in other states, probably like literally at the time I'm recording this video right now. and you don't pay a discount to be afforded the opportunity to do that. So, while Snowbird Life does reduce commitment risk, I think it often simultaneously increases total overhead. On the other side, if we kind of flip uh to the full-time Florida living equation, you eliminate the duplicate housing cost. You simplify the the financial flow mentally and fiscally upfront. And if you qualify, you can even throw in some benefits of long-term uh property tax advantages that just a partial resident wouldn't be allowed to do. But full-time also means that Florida is no longer optional. When you're here, you're here. You're here for hurricane season. Uh you're here for 12 months of the summer heat, not just for three. Uh insurance renewals, the volatility, all that kind of stuff. So, the real financial question isn't which is actually cheaper. It's probably uh more along the lines of do you want flexibility with higher overall total cost or would you prefer a full-on commitment with a clear long-term mathematical equation? Very lifestyle driven. It is linked in the description box below. It's meant to reduce overwhelm, not to push you to a decision. So check that out. And let's hop to number two. All right, on to category number two. The always fun subject of taxes, doicile, and just general gray era. This is the place where people I think can really quietly get themselves in trouble, especially if this is your first run at trying the snowbird thing. Many snowbirds believe in just one simple all-encompassing rule. If I stay under 183 days, I'm fine. And I think you really need to look at that because that belief at at very least is incomplete. The 183day rule is not necessarily universal and it is not the end- all beall deciding factor of where these states are considering you a resident from tax purposes. So a lot of the confusion to me basically starts with the idea that there's two completely different concepts that states use when determining residency. Most people like almost like merge them or talk about them like they're one conversation. The first one is uh probably probably the one you're familiar with and that's statutory residency. The second one would be the doicile. Statuto residency is basically where this 183 day uh number comes from. Some states say that if you're physically present in a state for more than 183 days during the year and you maintain a place there, they can treat you as a resident uh for tax purposes even if you claim to live somewhere else. So yes, staying under that 183day mark can help you and avoid triggering that particular statutory residency. But the key point to me is that avoiding statutory residence from a rule perspective doesn't automatically make you a non-resident. It just simply means that the state can't go after you for that one particular argument. Doicile is probably where the bigger issue actually lies because this is intent. And this is where snowbirds I think are most exposed. It's about where the state, not you, it's where the state believes your real permanent home is. And unfortunately, intent isn't proven by, you know, what you say, you explain, you argue, or anything like that. It's proven by how you live. And these guys look at everything. They look at the entire footprint. Where is your primary home? Which home is available to you year round? Uh where are your doctors and specialists? Where are your vehicles registered? Where do you vote? Where is your driver's license issued? You get the point here. You can keep going. Where are your financial adviserss? Uh where does your mail go to? Where do you return when something important happens? You can spend well under that 183day period in a state and still technically be considered doiciled there if your life just clearly points back to it. And that's the kind of like uh oh of why people are surprised when this comes up because they're like I wasn't even close to that mark. I was I stayed 78 days this year. And the state says it doesn't matter. That's not what we're arguing. Snowbirds are uniquely vulnerable here because it's how their lives are structured. They often uh keep primary homes up north. They have long-standing doctors and health care system relationships they've had forever. They keep their driver's license and voter registration unchanged. They follow the same predictable seasonal pattern every single year that can be tracked. So from a state perspective, it doesn't look like a relocation. It looks like someone who leaves for a temporary short period of time and then they come back when things matter. And this is where the dangerous gray area forms. You obviously feel like a flidian. You spend months here. You may you may even own property or two here, but your old state still sees you as theirs. It's unfortunate because you know kind of the juxaposition truth to me is uh feeling like a flidian on paper is not going to be treat considered or treated like one. Um, physically the most dangerous position is believing you really left your old state behind while your old state still believes that you belong to them. Full-time Florida residency can bring clarity to that. But I think only if it's also done intentionally and consistently. It's not just about the days. It's about the full kind of alignment of all of it. Your paperwork, your behavior, your lifestyle does it all tell the exact same story. It's kind of like in a weird way uh homestead hopping. Like one investment strategy of low liquidity is you buy the one 5% down, you live in that house, then you keep leaving it. You don't sell the old ones, right? So you still have that low liquidity jump. And the only reason that's not mortgage fraud is you have to have a physical reason to need to move. Um so that you're not kind of gaming the system. So look, a snowbird thing can absolutely work. Don't get me wrong, this isn't fear-mongering or anything like that, of course, but it works best when you accept what it is. It's seasonal living. It is not full relocation. Problems happen a lot when people just want to merge the two. That's when you end up paying for two lifestyles with none of the clarity. All right, number three on my list uh is kind of just like a relatively boring functional one. Often plays second fiddle to a lot of the other more exciting uh parts of this list, but I think it's worth mentioning, and that is healthcare, Medicare, and more importantly, the portability that comes with all these items. It's something you really don't even think about deeply until you're forced to. And the reason I bring it up is that healthcare does not automatically follow you depending on your particular situation because you own property somewhere else or you even spend a significant part of the year outside of your homestead based state depending on your age and insurance all that kind of stuff. Snowbird life versus full-time Florida residency can drift apart pretty uh aggressively. You know, if you're still working of course like at an employer and you're on one of those employer sponsored plans, they're a lot more national based. They give a lot of flexibility. So if if you happen to be in the in the few that are able to kind of bridge that gap, this won't be a big issue for you. But once you enter the Medicare kind of picture, uh I think things get a little squirly. And this uh is where a lot of the surprises in my personal experience have happened. Not all Medicare plans travel the same way. So the original, you know, the original Medicare with like the supplemental policy is generally the most portable I've seen. you you can receive care many places ac across the country without worrying as much about like the network specific stuff. But then when it comes to the Medicare Advantage plans of the world, a lot of times they're really regional. This is they're ideally kind of infrastructure at least based where uh they work best where you live full-time. The doctors, the hospitals, the specialists, all those referral systems in there. Uh many times they're built around one geographic market. So, it's just it's a one kind of caution tail that I would suggest that you look into this kind of early and often before you get excited about something that you know isn't going to benefit you as much. Now, the advantage of the full-time Florida residency in contrast to that would be that you can just align everything locally. - Snowbirding in Retirement? 5 Thing to Know Before You Do: Kind: captions Language: en Today on the Wealth Cafe, we are going to dive into what it means to be a snowbird. No, we're not changing our life form here. So, for those of you that may not know the term snowbird is typically people who in retirement, they will spend half the year in a warmer weather state. And so, typically, so I live in New Jersey. So, in New Jersey in the winter months, you typically see people who are moving down to Florida, Arizona. um they go to places that are warmer so they don't want to have to deal with the snow and the sle and all of the different weather forms that we have here during those colder months. And so what they also do is they make sure that they are strategically picking states where they want to go to that have a lower tax rate and that helps them during their retirement years. So in order to to do this though they need to make sure that they are spending just over 6 months in that state. So, example, if you were becoming a snowbird and going from New Jersey to Florida, you need to make sure you're just spending over 6 months in Florida to qualify for all of those tax deductions. However, there is so much more than going into snowboarding just for the money saving side of it. There's a lot of different factors to think about, and I find in a lot of my conversations with clients, they don't always think through what all goes into being a snowbird. And so it might be something of, you know what, we're going to make sure we live in a community that takes care of all of those things for us, right? they have the lawn care, the the pools are shared, so nobody has to worry about the the chlorine levels and getting it ready for this season. Keep in mind that's going to come with HOA fees. So, there's pros and cons of everything. - Top 10 Snowbird Destinations in the US for Winter Escapes: Kind: captions Language: en looking for the perfect winter Escape in the United States whether you're trying to avoid freezing temperatures or simply seeking some sunshine we've got you covered today we're counting down the 10 best Snowbird destinations in the US where you can enjoy warm weather fun activities and a change of pace from Coastal retreats to Desert oases these destinations are ideal for anyone wanting to swap snow for Sun during the colder months stick around to discover which spot could be your next winter home and wait until you hear about our number one pick it's a destination where the winter weather is so perfect it's almost like summer all year round destination 10 Sarasota Florida Sarasota offers a fantastic blend of white sand beaches a rich art scene and endless Sunshine located on Florida's Gulf Coast Sarasota is perfect for snowbirds who love Outdoor Living mixed with culture beyond the beaches the city is home to world world class museums like the wringling theater ballet and live music events year round it's the ideal spot for snowbirds who want relaxation and access to an upscale lifestyle renting a condo or apartment in Sarasota can be surprisingly affordable especially if you stay slightly outside the touristy areas with mild Winters averaging in the 70s you can spend your days golfing fishing or exploring the nearby barrier islands like Siesta Key and if you're a foodie Sarasota vibrant dining scene will keep you coming back every season destination 9 Tucson Arizona Tucson is calling your name if you're a fan of dramatic desert Landscapes and Outdoor Adventures situated in the heart of the sonor and desert Tucson offers warm sunny Winters and a rugged natural beauty that's hard to beat snowbirds love it for its hiking trails including the famous Saguaro National Park and it's also a prime destination for golfers with several top rated courses Tucson's cost of living is lower than in other popular Arizona Snowbird spots like Scottdale making it an attractive destination for budget conscious Travelers with average winter temperatures in the mid-60s to 7s it's perfect for anyone looking to trade in snow shovels for hiking boots and enjoy the southwest's natural wonders destination 8 Austin Texas if you're looking for a mix of culture music and Mild Winters Austin Texas should be on your list known as The World's live music Capital Austin offers a vibrant urban lifestyle without the freezing temperatures snowbirds flock to Austin for its eclectic art scene live music venues and incredible food trucks you can explore outdoor Parks like Zilker Park or enjoy the city's famous food festivals in Winter while Austin isn't the warmest on our list with winter temps in the 50s and 60s it's perfect for those who want to avoid harsh winter weather without losing out on an urban experience plus Austin's lower cost of living compared to major cities like Los Angeles or Miami makes it an appealing Snowbird destination for those who want big city amenities at a lower price destination 7 Hilton Head South Carolina if coastal living with A Touch of Southern Charm sounds appealing Hilton Head Island is the perfect Snowbird Retreat known for its stunning beaches world world class golf courses and Serene Landscapes Hilton Head offers a peaceful yet active Lifestyle the island is great for those who enjoy biking boating or simply relaxing by the water and the mild winter weather often in the 60s makes outdoor activities accessible year round Hilton Head is also known for its sense of community with plenty of clubs and social events tailored for snowbirds whether hitting the tennis courts strolling through local farmers markets or enjoying fresh seafood along the coast Hilton Head offers an upscale relaxed Vibe for those looking to escape the cold Destination six California Palm Springs is the quintessential Snowbird destination for those seeking Sunshine luxury and a vibrant Social Scene this Southern California desert city is famous for its mid-century modern architecture upscale resorts and endless Sunshine during winter temperatures hover in the comfortable 70s making it a favorite spot for snowbirds who love to golf hike or lounge by the pool beyond the sunshine Palm Springs is known for its festivals including the International Film Festival and modernism week attracting visitors from all over the world while Palm Springs can be pricier it offers a unique blend of relaxation and glamour making it a top tier choice for anyone seeking an upscale Snowbird Escape destination five St a August Florida St Augustine is a Hidden Gem among Snowbird destinations if you love history and Charming coastal towns as the oldest continuously inhabited European settlement in the US St Augustine offers centuries of history from its Spanish Colonial architecture to the famous Castillo de San Marcos but it's not just about history the town also offers miles of beautiful beaches cozy cafes and local art galleries Winters in St Augustine are mild with temperatures in the 60s and 7s making it perfect for strolling along the cobblestone streets or enjoying a beach day in January the town's affordable housing options and Rich cultural offerings make it an attractive destination for snowbirds looking for more than just sunny skies Destination 4 Las Vegas Nevada while Las Vegas may seem like an unusual Choice It's actually an excellent destination for snowbirds who enjoy entertainment dining and warm Winters temperatures in Las Vegas during winter average around 60 to 70° making it an ideal escape from colder climates and while the strip is the city's Main Attraction Vegas offers so much more for snowbirds including hiking at Red Rock Canyon and exploring the nearby desert Landscapes one of the biggest draws of Las Vegas is its affordability you can find surprisingly low rental prices especially in neighborhoods just outside the main tourist areas Plus with no state income tax and endless dining shopping and entertainment options Vegas provides a lifestyle that's hard to match elsewhere destination three Naples Florida Naples located on Florida's Gulf Coast is a Haven for snowbirds who want to indulge in luxury without enduring the cold Naples is known for its high-end shopping golf courses and Gourmet dining it is perfect for those seeking an upscale Snowbird destination the city's pristine beaches and calm Gulf Waters offer a peaceful Escape for retirees and snowbirds alike while Naples is known for its luxury it offers plenty of affordable housing options especially for seasonal snowbirds the weather in Naples is unbeatable with winter temperatures often hovering in the 70s and 80s allowing for yearr round outdoor activities like boating fishing and golfing destination 2 Phoenix Arizona Phoenix is a Desert Paradise for snowbirds seeking warmth outdoor activities and affordability with winter temperatures averaging in the mid-60s to 7s Phoenix offers the perfect climate for hiking golfing or simply soaking up the sun snowbirds love Phoenix for its stunning desert Landscapes with easy access to Outdoor Adventures like Camelback Mountain and the nearby Superstition Mountains Phoenix's cost of living is lower than in other Snowbird hotspots and it offers a wide range of housing options from affordable cont to gated retirement communities with a growing food scene Rich cultural history and plenty of social events tailored to seasonal residents Phoenix remains one of the most popular Snowbird destinations in the United States topping our list is San Diego California a city that offers near perfect weather all year round with its endless beaches vibrant neighborhoods and Rich cultural scene San Diego is the dream destination for snow Birds who want to escape winter but enjoy an active urban lifestyle Winters in San Diego rarely drop below the 60s and with Sunshine nearly every day it's the ultimate place to trade your winter coat for flip-flops San Diego's diverse offerings from the trendy Gaslamp Quarter to laidback Beach communities like La Hoya provide something for every type of snowbird while it can be more expensive than other destinations the city's lifestyle weather and activities make it a Top Choice for a - 10 Cheap & Warm Countries to Move in Winters (Snowbirds): Kind: captions Language: en some people want to embrace the winter season by seeking out cold snowy destinations while others want to escape the season by jetting off to sunny beaches and tropical islands if you fall into the latter category you're in luck we've rounded up some of the best budget-friendly winter sun destinations where the temperatures rarely drop below 30 degrees celsius regardless of the season the countries mentioned in this video are some of the best to visit in december january and february to get some sun being outside allows you to stretch your legs and exercise as well as be out in nature both proven ways to improve health and mental performance from the white sand beaches of the south pacific to the breathtaking coastlines of the indian ocean there are so many great winter escapes you can spend your time in so here are 10 cheap and warm countries to move in winters number 10 dominican republic the dominican republic is popular with snowbirds seeking an inexpensive destination for fun under the sun as its far eastern edge you'll find the collection of beach towns that are collectively referred to as punta cana unlike most of the other caribbean islands there are lots of massive beach accommodation available in this area that allows them to offer some of the best rates in the caribbean punta cana has colorful resorts on bavaro beach with lots of modern amenities including satellite tv and free wi-fi while you're here in addition to all of the usual beach activities you can take a day trip to indigenous eyes park a private nature reserve and jungle park with waterfalls and lagoons for swimming or explore the cordillera mountains which is the highest mountain range in the caribbean number nine thailand the beaches of thailand are a true paradise with tropical climate in winter months the heat is less suffocating and there are in general fewer tourists it actually became a favorite destination for digital nomads as well while the touristy beach areas in the southern region of thailand like phuket are pricey by heading north on the overnight train from bangkok to chiang mai you'll not only enjoy a more authentic experience but cheap accommodations as well as the chance to enjoy the area's magnificent mountains vibrant hill tribes and a more relaxed vibe the weather is ideal with warm sunny days and mild nights while rustic but clean comfortable and private rooms can be had for under ten dollars a night number eight mauritius mauritius is an island nation in the indian ocean about 1800 kilometers from the african mainland the green mountains of the island and its white beaches arise from clear blue waters setting foot on the shore is like stepping into a dream but mauritius offers more than just tropical beaches the country welcomes expats with communities from many nations and many mauritian visas and residence permits lead to mauritius citizenship and a passport it's the safest country in africa and ranks highly on the global peace index so in general you can live a life without worrying about the safety the best months to go to mauritius are september october and november when it's hot but not too hot averaging around 25 to 32 degrees celsius in the day a cooler 16 to 19 degrees celsius at night number seven panama panama has a tropical monsoon climate fortunately it is located below the hurricane belt and is not directly affected by them few people put panama on their list of places to move during the winters but it not only offers an ideal destination for warm weather and sunshine it also boasts some of the safest budget-friendly areas there are several beach areas expanding out from the main city with large condo developments popping up along this pacific stretch of coastline on the flip side it will cost you a bit more to get there due to the lesser demand but unlike high traffic destinations such as bahamas once you get there it's not only a lot more economical it's less likely to be overrun with tourists number six saint martin saint martin is part of the leeward islands in the caribbean sea winter is the best time to visit st martin and these are times when accommodation is more affordable than usual snowbirds will also find that the island is home to busy resort beaches and secluded coves it's also known for fusion cuisine vibrant nightlife and duty-free shops selling jewelry and liquor the climate of saint martin is tropical hot and sunny all year round the average daily temperature ranges from 25 degrees celsius to 28 degrees celsius furthermore it is generally considered a safe place to visit but as with any destination snowbirds should be aware petty theft does still exist here as it does nearly everywhere number five portugal winter in portugal is vastly different to spending time here during the peak summer months lower prices tasty comfort food winter festivals and the promise of warm sunny days are just a few of the perks of traveling at this time of year but keep in mind inland central portugal can experience extremes of temperature and can see snow in the depths of winter however for those further south winter days temperature peaks around 30 degrees celsius and if you want to experience more hotter temperatures you can head to the portuguese island of madeira a great winter destination you may not have considered at all enjoy local seafood exotic fruits and the island's famous namesake fortified wine as well as its year-round gorgeous spring-like weather number four sri lanka sri lanka is a tropical island with the potential to be your perfect winter destination lush palm trees lined along its coastline that seemed to never end abundant wildlife and almost heavenly weather also play a key role in why so many snowbirds choose to spend their winter here sri lanka is the 25th largest island on the planet and is nestled on the southwest end of the indian ocean between the arabian sea and the bay of bengal furthermore the country is considered a very safe travel destination while sri lanka is still recovering from a decades-long civil war the people are warm and friendly moreover the cost of living in this country is very cheap and a couple can easily live here on a monthly budget of fifteen hundred dollars number three colombia winter is when colombia home to sandy beaches vast rain forests beautiful snow-capped mountains fantastic coffee and charming colonial cities gets its very best weather with average high temperatures at 30 degrees celsius at average lows of 20 degrees celsius it's sunny and warm but not too hot and it's far cheaper than any other spot in the caribbean the high season ends in mid-december so the already affordable accommodation rates will be even less expensive from mid-december through the end of february most of the people head to the city of cartagena in winter months a city on the northern coast facing the caribbean features impressive mangroves powdery beaches and brilliant coral reefs along with a historic old town surrounded by the city wall number two mexico mexico has long been known as - Snowbird Resort Guide | Utah | USA: Kind: captions Language: en Snowbird is the biggest of the four resorts in the Cottonwood Canyons just outside Salt Lake City. The resort has a respectable 2 and a half thousand skiable acres and receives over 500 in of snow each year. Some years that can exceed 700, rivaling Japan. If you're a skier, there's the option to drop over into Alter for even more terrain. There is a limited range of lodging options at the base, but loads of options down the road in the city. Snowbird has a few green trails on the lower mountain for beginners and a few blue trails scattered across the resort for intermediate skiers. The extent of black, double black, and tree skiing zones makes this mountain an advanced skiers playground. Beginners and less confident intermediate skiers will find themselves constrained to certain trails or areas. Advanced skiers will be more focused on avoiding lift cues in order to take advantage of what Snowbird has to offer. Snowbird is roughly 45 minutes from the airport or half an hour's drive from the eastern suburbs of Salt Lake City when the road is clear. This can increase dramatically due to weather or traffic. On weekends or powder days, you'll need to leave early to avoid spending hours in traffic or even worse, not being able to park. Check DOT for traffic and road conditions before you leave Salt Lake City. >> There are numerous private transfer options from the airport to and from your lodging. There is also public transport up to Snowbird, but no dedicated transit lane, so the bus gets stuck in traffic, but this may be the only option when there are parking issues up the canyon. If you are driving, we recommend a four-wheel or all-wheel drive, and you will need winter tires as required by Utah law. Most of the parking at Snowbird is free, but requires strategic planning, especially on weekends and coveted powder days when the lots fill up early, potentially leaving you without a spot. Car pooling is rewarded with three plus people guided to prime locations. For a parking guarantee, consider paid reservations or valet and book online. The main base center plaza is an awesome structure with a distinct dystopian flavor. The facilities inside are wellappointed and modern with friendly staff who are welcoming. All required guest services, including ski passes, rentals, lockers, and ski school are available in the center plaza. There is also lift access from Creekide, so day visitors with passes don't need to go to the main center. If you are parked between the center and creek side, there is a free shuttle that will take you to either base area. There are only two on- mountain food options. The summit has a restaurant and cafeteria with spectacular views on a clear day. Midgad is being overhauled, but was a great mid-mount venue, albeit very busy between 10 and 2. The center base area has a few food options, while Creek Side offers an alternative a little removed from the main crowds. >> Let's get to the skiing. Snowbird is a playground for advanced skiers, while beginners will find themselves constrained to the lower slopes, and intermediates have limited options all across the resort. >> For the first time, a tricky provides a secluded learning area away from the main ski traffic. Beginners venturing onto the mountain are limited to slow fixed grip lifts servicing the lower slopes like Miners Road and Middle Emma. There are a few trails like Ellis Avenue and Thunder Junction off the Baby Thunder for the adventurous beginner, but you will need to level up to blue trails if you plan to experience any more of this resort. The Mini Miners Camp is a fun adventurous zone to entertain the kids. For the intermediate skier, there is at least one blue trail off each of the express lifts, although some of them are traversing tracks that may test the nerves of less confident skiers. Banana and Election Blue Trails of Gad 2 provide winding trails away from the main ski traffic. Great for building confidence and skills. Basacquids and Emma are long trails that can be busy in certain sections. Chips Run is an epic long rolling blue, a musto trail if visiting Snowbird. Over on Mineral Basin, there are a few enjoyable blues, but expect traffic on the lower sections as skiers are funneled towards the two lifts. Advanced skiers will find trails and tree areas across the resort, but most of the technical skiing requires a traverse to access. For some bowl skiing, drop off path to paradise onto trails like Lonear and Nash Flora Load. The lower sections like Guin Gully get quite technical. A traversal on book ends accesses some long steep trails, but there is a run out, so keep your speed at the bottom. On the front side, regulator Johnson provides a taste of bowl skiing while Shireen and Mark Malu Fork both lead into Goblin Gully, which is a bit of fun. It's possible to do quick laps on the upper slopes off Little Cloud if the lift isn't busy. >> STH and Gazuks off the Gad 2 lift are fairly straightforward blacks, while Black Forest and Organ Grinder provide a bit of excitement and some steep sections. There are some steep and technical trails under the tram like upper Silver Fox and Upper Circ. Taking circ traverse which is tricky in places and might have you burning a PEX candle afterwards gets you to some fantastic long technical trails like Dolton's draw and Wilbur bowl trails on the lower slopes like blackjack and route four gullies will make sure you've earned the rest on the tram ride back up. >> There are a few short blacks offg zoom like sneaky pete and carbonate but the blue or green runout makes this lift more of a transfer route to the little cloud and gad to lifts if you want to get away from the crowds. Tiger Tail and Mozzarella Bowl are fun, while Tiny Tiger and Thunder Alley are even more secluded, but you'll be riding three lifts to get back up, including the slow Baby Thunder grip. >> There are no terrain parks at Snowbird, but the many gullies and rollers provide natural features across the resort for anyone looking for some aerial antics. >> There are a few things to be aware of for Snowbird. Mineral Basin faces south. Great for soaking up the sun on a cold January day, but it means the snow is sunbaked and patrol must manage avalanche risks. The north facing front side tends to have better snow conditions. Snow Bed has some years where more than 700 in is received, which further impacts snow conditions on mountain and can make Little Cottonwood Canyon a coin flip between accidents or avalanches. Getting stuck on the road for hours is not uncommon. While being trapped for a few days and sleeping in CS in the center is not unheard of. When this happens, staying on the mountain has distinct benefits. But don't anticipate much of the resort to be open after a massive storm. Expect early morning lift cues on the aerial tram, Peruvian and Gadoo, as these are the main lifts from the base. On weekends and powder days, there's a queue for the tram almost all day. On occasion, we've used the slow midgad lift because the queue for gads was horrendous. From midm morning, the queue at Little Cloud kicks in. And when the weather is good, the cues for the baldy and mineral basin lifts can be painful. The extent of terrain and volume of skiers serviced by a handful of lifts make lift cues the Achilles heel of Snowbird. >> For the best mix of snow conditions and reduced crowds, late January into February is the best time to visit. But the resort is busy weekends and powder days all season. March brings epic snow, but it also brings the spring breakers. >> It's worth noting snowboarders will find some of the traversing trails that access the steeps challenging. The rocks and mini rollers make holding your speed tricky, but it's a necessary evil as some of the best terrain is accessed this way. When the weather sets in, the Peruvian and Gadu lifts can be a little sheltered from the wind. Plus, the trees provide reference points to follow the trails in low visibility. On good days when everyone is over in Mineral Basin or on Little Cloud, these two lifts can have shorter cues. The best place to escape the crowds is across at Baby Thunder. The terrain is limited, but it's better to be skiing than queuing. >> If you are driving up, keep an eye on the weather and UD do as road closures are common. On weekends, leave extra early to avoid traffic and get parking. Plus, consider ending your day a little early to get home without hassle. If there's a storm rolling in, be prepared to call it a day before it arrives so you don't get stuck up the valley. Skiers on an icon pass or wanting to explore beyond snowbird can ski across to Alta for added terrain and an escape from snowboarders. There are snow cap and hely skiing tours run from the resort for anyone looking to take full advantage of the epic Utah snow. >> There are a few lodging options offered through Snowbird Resort, plus a number of private condos in the valley. Staying up in the valley is a lot more expensive than the options down in Salt Lake City. And availability is also an issue. Most private lodging is booked out months in advance. The ski in ski out experience avoids the daily drive, but does come with a risk of being snowed in for a day or more. There are a few restaurants in the resort base area and a couple of convenience stores in the main center. For major grocery supplies, a trip down the valley or an online delivery may provide more options. If you are staying a little further from base, there is a bus service in the village. >> In our opinion, Snowbird is a resort for advanced and expert skiers who enjoy steep or technical terrain along with some challenging trails for confident intermediates. The mix of bold trail and tree skiing across the resort will test skills at every corner. Beginners will find the resort constrained and will need to step up to blues to see more than the lower slopes. >> The weekend crowds and lift cues might frustrate some. While the potential of being snowed in for a few days may provide stories to tell friends and family for years to come, Snowbird is a resort full of surprises and likely to provide a ski experience hard to forget. Check out our trail and lift videos to get the most out of your visit to this resort.

- More Than Just A Snowbird: the story of the Canadair CT-114 Tutor: Kind: captions Language: en-CA The snowbirds are the most visible component of the Royal Canadian Air Force. Painted in their iconic red and white pattern, they tour North America putting on stunning aerial displays. The workhorse behind the snowbirds for over 30 years is the humble the Canadair CT-114 Tutor, a high performance jet trainer with a long history with the RCAF. The Tutor was the first in house design for Canadair. Based in Montreal, Canadair had until then made a name for themselves building and upgrading foreign designs. These included the CL-30 Shooting Star trainer, the CL-13 Sabre fighter, and the C-4 North Star transport. In the early 1950s Canada was operating jet aircraft like the Sabre and the Canuck but new aircraft were being developed at an enormous rate. Higher performance aircraft with expanded flight profiles like the upcoming American Starfighter demonstrated the need for an expanded training program for pilots. Canadair initiated a two seat jet trainer program with the intention of replacing the existing propeller driven fleet and to keep pace with advancements. It was hoped that the design would be completed ahead of the anticipated contracts from the RCAF and other allied air forces for a new jet powered lead-in trainer. There were other designs being developed concurrently by countries including the United States and the United Kingdom. Aircraft like the popular Cessna T-37 Tweet and the BAC Jet Provost had their first flights in 1954, around the same time the Tutor was being designed. Despite this late start Canadair was confident that they could compete in the allied market for jet trainers. In February of 1951, designer karl Irbitis, who would later go one to design the innovative CL-84 vertical take off and landing swing-wing aircraft, proposed the first version was simply called the Jet Trainer. It was to be produced in several versions to reflect the operational requirements. In the primary trainer role the Tutor was supposed to have a wide flight envelope in order to replace both the Harvard and CT-133 Silver Star trainers already being used. The initial design series was oriented towards perfecting the spin initiation and recovery performance to align it with the training The CL-41-1 was proposed in January of 1954. It had most of the features present in the final production design but had a low set tail wing. Wind tunnel tests found this configuration to be too sensitive to student inputs when entering and exiting a spin and so the design was revised. The CL-41-2 had a T-tail and went through many iterations between 1954 and 1956 to determine its optimal position along the tail rudder. Using models in a wind tunnel, the wing was raised incrementally from its low set position to the familiar T-tail configuration to determine the best placement. In Jan of 1956, the CL-41-3 was proposed. It had a tandem seat configuration similar to that of the Harvard and CT-133. It was not pursued beyond paper studies due to the fact that the RCAF had indicated that they wanted a side by side trainer. The CL-41-4 was a further modified version of the CL-41-2 model. It included a larger tail rudder, increased cord for the vertical fin and refinements surrounding the placement of the tail wing. This model was tested extensively and showed the desired flight characteristics. By mid-1956 wooden mockups were built and the design was more or less finalised and Canadair began soliciting governments to fund a flying prototype. Funding for the construction of two prototypes was secured in Sept 1956. The government of Canada decided to jointly fund the project alongside Canadair and the project began to pick up momentum. The CL-41A, was designed to be well suited to the lead-in trainer role. It first flew on January 13th 1960. Performance of the new jet was respectable and compared favorably to its contemporaries. The tutor consisted of two main all-metal semi-monocoque structural units connected by four bolts. The forward section included the nose, cockpit and center fuselage, while the rear section included the engine and tail surfaces. The wings were mated to the center fuselage. A pair of speed brakes were located halfway down the tail section. This configuration enabled easy engine replacement and maintenance. 45 panels around the aircraft provided ground crews with easy access for any maintenance needs. The first prototype was equipped with a Pratt & Whitney built JT12A-1 engine. Later this would be replaced by the Orenda-built General Electric J85-CAN-49 two stage turbine producing 1,180kg of thrust. This propelled the 3,357kg aircraft to a top speed of 888 km/h up to a maximum altitude of 13,100m. The Tutor had a wingspan of 11.13m, a length of 9.75m, and stood 2.82m at the tail. The side-by-side cockpit was large and spacious with a wraparound rear hinged canopy. It seated the student on the left and the instructor on the right. This configuration allowed students to directly observe the examples given by the instructor as well as allowing the instructor to directly evaluate the progress of the student. The crew sat on a pair of Weber ROCAT ejection seats which allowed a zero-altitude, zero-speed ejection. The tutor featured a wide wheel base for good directional stability on the ground and a robust undercarriage to resist rough landings. It also incorporated an internal skid framework to help protect the crew in the event of a belly landing. 1137 L worth of fuel tanks were located around the center of gravity in the main fuselage to maintain consistent flight characteristics regardless of fuel load. It had a range of 1097 km on internal tanks but this could be extended through the use of two 455L external wingtip tank. Due to the requirement that it be able to operate from older and less prepared runways, the first two Tutor prototype were fitted with retractable air intake screens to protect the engine from ingesting foreign object debris. These screens would cover the intakes during landings, taxing, and take offs, and then retract behind a boundary layer deflector when in flight to minimize drag. These were later scraped on the production version but were brought back with the exported G model. Weapon mounts were included but were not used initially. These consisted of two under-fuselage mount along with four under-wing pylon mounts on each wing. It was intended that combinations of fuel tanks, guns, bombs, or rockets could be carried and deployed in the training role. Later these too would make a come back with the G model. Throughout the design crew safety, crew communication, ease of maintenance were top priorities. However performance was not forgotten. The airframe was designed to take between -3 and +7Gs, this combined with a good rate of climb made the Tutor a solid aerobatic platform. This fact was not lost on the RCAF who mounted smoke generators to the exhaust and went on to use the Toot for most of its display teams. Canadair, and later the RCAF, would perform extensive structural test on the airframes to ensure that they would be able to handle this aerobatic strain safely. Flight testing of the first prototype continued while a second was produced. The second prototype was built to be a stripped down cheaper version of the A model. Canadair intended to make their trainer as appealing as possible to as many foreign countries as possible. The lower cost B model was meant to make it more accessible to less well financed air forces. Modifications included replacing the engine with an Armstrong Siddeley Viper and combining and minimizing the cockpit instruments. The idea was abandoned and the company renewed its focus on the potentially lucrative contracts of providing radar training to the many upcoming F-104 pilots. By 1959, Canadair had begun the slight modification and construction of around 340 F-104 Starfighters. It was this contract that led designers at Canadair to consider the creation of a new lead-in trainer for the high performance jets. The single seat Starfighter and was expected to have low airframe hours requiring an efficient way to transition pilots onto the new type. The company wanted to fill the need for radar and all-weather navigation training and so began incorporating additional systems into the 41s design. The B model was upgraded to the CL-41R standard through extensive airframe modifications and the inclusion of a R24A North American Search and Ranging Radar, or NASARR, and its associated equipment. The redesigned Toot was lengthened by adding a totally new nose section, and had two side blisters along the tail boom to hold the additional equipment required to support the radar set. These blisters also provided a counter-balance for the heavy radar. The engine was upgraded to a Pratt & Whitney JT12A-2. The engine, along with all the extra radar equipment, brought the gross weight up to 3,765kgs, this was heavier than expected and had an impact on performance. The engine was later upgraded again to an Orenda built J85-J4. The performance of the R model was similar to the A but with a slightly reduced top speed and altitude. This model was cancelled due to lack of interest at home and abroad. The role of F-104 radar training for the RCAF was left to three Dakota transports. These were modified to include the NASARR systems and a Starfighter-style nose cone. The CL-41R airframe was then used for vibration and fatigue testing. It now resides in storage at the Reynolds-Alberta Museum, in Wetaskiwin, Alberta. The RCAF was pleased with the Tutor’s performance and they placed an order for 190 aircraft in April 1962. The first production model CL-41A was rolled out of the factory in Montreal on October 29th 1963. The only initial change from the production version was the exclusion of the FOB screens. By 1964, 4 were being produced per month, later a Toot would roll off the production line every 5 days. Several production techniques were used that would later help build the world fastest hydrofoil, the HMCS Bras d'Or, such as mounting the airframes in a jig that reduced workload by easily rotating to any orientation. A modification was proposed during production to correct some of the aerodynamics of the Tutor and improve its spin recovery characteristics. Narrow spin strakes were mounted on either side of the nose to guide airflow at various angles of attack. Almost all the CL-41s were upgraded to include these strakes, the notable exceptions being early production airframes and those used as part of the RCAF’s Golden Centennaires. The CL-41A Tutor, renamed the CT-114 Tutor by the RCAF, entered service on December 16th 1963. Several of the first airframes were attributed to various units for testing and maintenance training. The first operational Tutors were assigned to Training Command at RCAF Gimli in Manitoba and began training pilots in 1963. The first class of pilots graduated in late 1964 and began transitioning to other types. The training program was expanded as more airframes became available. Instructor training was concentrated in Manitoba at the RCAF Portage la Prairie #1 Flying Instructor School. There instructors would familiarise themselves with the aircraft and refine their teaching skills. Primary flight training for student pilots would be concentrated with the #2 Flight Training Squadron, later renamed the #2 Canadian Forces Flying Training School, at RCAF Moose Jaw, in Saskatchewan. The vast majority of Tutors would be based there. The 2 FTS would train all fast jet pilots in Canada as well as a large number of pilots from other countries as part of Canada’s contribution to NATO and other allies. Pilots came from all over the world including Tanzania, Malaysia, Italy, Denmark, Norway, and the Netherlands. The public got to see the new plane in late 1964 at an air show in Toronto. The Tutor was displayed at various other national and international air shows before an air demonstration team could be assembled. The first display team was the Golden Centennaires, formed in 1966 as part of the centennial celebrations. It consisted of 10 modified Tutors painted in a distinctive gold, dark blue and red scheme. They performed from April 28th to November 18th 1967 and was disbanded in January of 1968. In May of 1968 the Red Knight display team was re-equipped with the Tutor following a crash of one of their T-33s. Two airframes were painted red with a white stripe down the fuselage and were used for demonstrations starting in the summer of 1968. Unfortunately a fatal crash in July of 1969 forced an end to the program. Starting in 1976, instructors from the Flying Instructors School based in CFB Moose Jaw began demonstration flights with 4 or 5 unpainted aircraft. They were known as the “Vikings Formation Display Team” and performed at various airshows throughout north america. The group was moved along with the #2 FIS from CFB Moose Jaw to CFB Portage la Prairie and then disbanded in 1984. The most famous Canadian display team is without question the Snowbirds. They were formed in 1969 when they began doing flypasts at Saskatchewan Roughrider football games. These proved to be very popular and so they began touring airshows in 1970. In 1971 they were named the Snowbirds and then in 1975 they officially became the 431 Air Demonstration Squadron, but the old name stuck in the minds of the public. As did the distinctive paint scheme which was introduced between 1974 and 1976. The team operates 11 planes but only uses 9 of them during performances. Snowbird pilots are selected from other squadrons and train together intensively before taking their show on the road. Mistakes and malfunctions can have fatal consequences when planes are this close or closing at such high speeds. Tragically 8 pilots and crew have been killed flying as part of the 431, 4 of which were in front of the public. There have also been several non-fatal crashes. Even with these tragedies the Snowbirds continue to fly and pilots continue to volunteer to participate in such a fantastic tradition of aerial mastery. Canadair had been actively promoting the Tutor to foreign air forces since the late 50s but with little success. The expected orders for the plane from allied air forces were filled by competitors like the Jet Provost and T-37 Tweet. In 1964 the company began to promote a combat version for use in weapons training and light attack roles. The Tutor had been design from the outset to be capable of carrying external stores on 6 pylons, two under the fuselage and two under each wing. A CL-41A was brought back to the Canadair factory and was remade into the CL-41G prototype by adding a reflector gunsight for the pilot, a 16mm gun camera, and strengthened rough field landing gear. Foreign object intake screens were planned but not included on the prototype. The CL-41G began weapons trials in New Brunswick and RCAF Chatham. Further testing was then carried out in California and Florida where the Toot was shown to be able to deliver a large diversity of weapons with great accuracy. The weapons tested include bombs, 2.75 inch and 5 inch rockets, 7.62 mm gun pods, napalm, cluster bombs, and fire bombs. The plane was shown to representatives from the US, mexico, Ecuador, Columbia, Australia and New Zealand, but they showed little interest as many similar platforms were on offer. However Canadair did find some success in an unlikely place. The Malaysian air force was interested in a combat version for use as a counter-insurgency and interdiction aircraft as well as as a trainer. Canadair modified the 41A according to Malaysian specifications and created the CL-41G Tebuan, which is Malay for Wasp. 20 copies were produced between 1966 and 1967 with deliveries starting in July of 1967. The Tebuan saw combat against counter-state militias in the jungles near the border with Thailand. None were lost in combat but several later crashed on various training missions as the plane aged. The last of the Tebuan’s were retired from Malaysian service in 1986. A civil version was also considered. This would have been used either as civilian pilot training or modified with an expanded cockpit to hold four passengers. It was intended that this model be used for high speed executive transport but the concept was not pursued due to lack of interest and possible competition in this category from the Cessna T-37A Tweet, renamed the Cessna Model 407. Like the civil version, the following versions were never pursued past the pencil and paper stage but showed the great potential of the little Tutor. The CL-41J was a proposed VTOL version that incorporated three small jet engines behind the canopy. A large door covered the engines when the plane was in forward flight. The CL-41K was meant to be low level reconnaissance version. It mounted 3 Vinten cameras in the nose along side a terrain following radar. The CL-41N series was intended to be a US carrier trainer and sported reinforced landing gear and a strengthened rear fuselage to support an arresting hook. The CL-41T would have been a complete redesign for ground attack and troop support missions. It had two engines, terrain following radar, and 8 external hardpoints for weapons and fuel tanks. By the 1980s Canadian Tutor’s were beginning to show their age. In 1980 the glass windshields of the Tutors were replaced with those made from polycarbonate. This was done to increase the Tutor’s resiliency with respect to bird strikes. While this somewhat modernised the Toot, further modifications were required. Communication equipment and other additional systems were needed for its training mission. In addition to this the aircrafts avionics were aging and could no longer be supported. An upgrade program was put in place in 1985 to extend the life of the hard working jet past the turn of the century. VHF communication equipment was installed to bring the Tutor up to compliance with commercial aircraft flying into civilian airports. The Tutor also received a UHF transceiver, dual channel intercoms, solid state power inverters, and a new blade-style antenna behind the cockpit canopy. Even with these upgrades the systems onboard the Tutors was starting to age. After 37 years of service the Tutor was largely retired from service with the RCAF in 2000. The notable exceptions being those still flying with the Snowbirds. The airframes were retired to RCAF Mountain View, in Ontario where they joined a mothballed air force. This abundance of lower hour airframes is one of the reasons the Snowbirds have been able to continue flying for so long. They were replaced by CT-156 Harvard II and the CT-155 Hawk as the RCAF’s primary jet Canadair moved on from the success of their CL-41 and produce other successful in house designs like the swing-wing CL-84 Dynavert, and the CL-89 and CL-289 surveillance drones. It would also continue to modify and produce many aircraft for the Canadian armed forces such as the supersonic CF-5, which would provide both light attack and advanced training roles. The humble Tutor, or Toot as it was affectionately called, trained every fast aircraft pilot in the RCAF from the mid-1960 through past the turn of the century. But it wasn’t just Canadians who benefited from its outstanding training qualities, the Tutor trained hundreds of pilots from other NATO and friendly countries. During its service the Tutor was involved with 67 accidents resulting in the destruction of 65 airframes and the deaths of 22 pilots and students. Today only the snowbirds continue to fly the jet and as the airframes age and fewer remain available, they too will eventually retire one of Canada’s most iconic jets.
