all down for you you'll learn how the US substantial presence test works and why it's crucial for snowbirds what steps you can take to avoid being classified as a US tax resident the importance of IRS form 8840 and how it can save you from unnecessary tax obligations potential pitfalls like fbar requirements penalties in even State specific rules stay tuned because we're giving you everything you need to know to navigate the Snowbird lifestyle without getting caught in a web of tax problems let's dive in under us tax law Canadians who spend an extended amount of time in the US may be classified as resident aliens for us tax purposes if they meet the substantial presence test this test calculates your presence in the US over a 3-year period using the following formula count all the days spent in the US in the current year add onethird of the days spent in the the previous year add 1 16th of the days spent in the year before that if the total equals or exceeds 183 days you will be considered a US resident alien and may be required to file a US tax return form 1040 for example if you spend 150 days in 2024 100 days in 2023 and 50 days in 2022 in the US that would be days in this example the individual exceeds the 183-day threshold and would be required to file as a resident alien with the IRS snowbirds can avoid us tax residency by demonstrating a closer connection to Canada through IRS form 8840 closer connection exception statement for aliens this form establishes that despite meaning the substantial presence test you maintain stronger residential economic and personal ties to C Canada factors that demonstrate a closer connection to Canada include maintaining a permanent home in Canada having family members dependence and financial ties in Canada retaining memberships for example clubs or social organizations and personal property in Canada spending the majority of the year in Canada filing form 8840 must be done annually and failure to do so could result in the IRS treating you as a US resident alien for tax purposes with all the Associated Tax obligations reporting foreign assets with the IRS if you're classified as a US resident alien you're required to report your worldwide income and disclose any foreign Financial accounts under the forign bank account reporting fbar regulations the fbar filing threshold is triggered if the total value of your foreign accounts exceeds $10,000 at any time during the year failure to file the F Bar can result in steep penalties starting at $10,000 per account per year for non-compliance even if the failure is unintentional impact on Canadian businesses in addition to the fbar considerations snowbirds who operate Canadian businesses while spending time in the US face additional reporting obligations for instance if you own a Canadian Corporation you may need to file IRS form 5471 to report your ownership and activities related to the business non-compliance with form 5471 reporting carries significant penalties starting at $10,000 per year staying below the substantial presence threshold to avoid triggering us tax residency snowbirds are advised to limit their time in the US to fewer than 122 days per year this ensures that their total over 3 years does not meet or exceed the 183-day threshold keeping a detailed record of your days in the US including entry and exit dates is crucial as US Customs in Border Protection tracks this information electronically and shares it with the IRS if requested additional considerations one additional thing you'll want to be aware of is State specific tax obligations even if you avoid US federal tax residency certain US states such as Florida or California may have their own residency rules that could impact you the snowbird dilemma highlights the need for careful planning to balance time in the US with the desire to retain Canadian residency and avoid us tax obligations by understanding and adhering to the substantial presence test filing the appropriate IRS forms and maintaining strong ties to Canada snowbirds can enjoy their time abroad without encountering unnecessary tax complications for tailored advice consult a tax professional familiar with crossb tax issues thank you for watching this video and if you have any questions about snor bir status don't hesitate to reach out my contact information is listed below in - Medicare for Snowbirds  (and RV travel): Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I am Matthew Claassen with MedigapSeminars.org in this video we're gonna go over all you need to know about Medicare for Snowbirds, for RV'ers, as well as for people who are on Medicare and expecting to move to a different state. MedigapSeminars.org; An Independent Insurance Broker Specializing in Medicare. Are you a Snowbird; a person who has homes in more than one state and tends to move south to a warmer climate in the winter? Or perhaps you're in RV'er in a motor home and like to travel all over the country. Or you're simply a person on Medicare and expect to move. You want to know how that's going to impact your Medicare what decisions you have to make. If so, then you should have questions about how being a Snowbird or an RV'er or moving impacts your Medicare. What kind of decisions you need to make and what your Medicare rights are. That is what we will discuss in this video, and we are going to approach it sort of a frequently asked questions (FAQ) format. We handle Medicare questions and we handle Medicare for people literally coast-to-coast, from California to Virginian, and from Michigan to Miami. We are based in South Florida and because of that we tend to get a lot of questions from Florida Snowbirds or people moving down south, and it has become an area of expertise. The first question we tend to get, a fairly simple one; "what is the right Medicare plan for a person who's in multiple states during the year?" The answer to that question isn't so much what is the right plan as it is what is the wrong plan. When you sign up for Medicare, once you've enrolled, you get a red white and blue card that shows you have Medicare ... Medicare Part A and Medicare Part B. That is called "Original Medicare". That original Medicare, along with any supplement that you get with that, is good in every state. You can go to any doctor, any medical facility as long as they accept Medicare, anywhere in the country. You don't need to have a Physician give you permission to see a specialist. You have complete control over your Medicare. The other route you can take is enrolling in a Medicare Advantage plan. Medicare Advantage plans are typically HMO's and PPO's. There are some other types, but most are HMO's and PPO's. That's local coverage! That tends to be just you county, or just the counties surrounding your area year. If you've chosen an HMO, then you have no coverage, outside of emergency services, outside of your network. None whatsoever. You've lost your right to have insurance outside of your network. If you have a PPO, then you will have coverage outside of your network, but it will be at a higher cost. With the PPO you can see a doctor either in network, or out of network. In network, which tends to be your local area, you will have copays and deductibles and a maximum annual out of pocket expense of no more than $6700 a year (for 2017). If you go out of network it's gonna cost you more. It will cost you higher copays or higher deductibles, and your maximum annual out-of-pocket can be as high as $10,000 per year. There are regional PPO's that can offer some extended in-network coverage out-of-state, but that's beyond the scope of this particular video. The bottom line is that if you are a Snowbird or you're in RV'er, you want to avoid the Medicare Advantage plans. They don't typically satisfy your needs because you have multiple states with which you might want to have health insurance coverage. They will come back and say; but we have emergency services outside of our network anywhere in the country. Very true, but you have to read the fine print on an emergency service. Often times an emergency service is defined as simply stabilizing you until your life is no longer threatened. A perfect example is someone's traveling out of state and gets in an automobile accident and is in traction with almost everything broken. Emergency service is making sure that you're alive and stable. After that you have to get yourself back to your service area for coverage. You have to get back in-network. Otherwise it is out-of-network coverage and that can be very, very expensive. If you're a Snowbird or RV'er you would want to avoid Medicare Advantage plans. If however you find that the Medicare supplements are simply too expensive for you, or the average Medicare supplement, then you might want to consider something called Medicare Supplement Plan F - high deductible. Or, as will be introduced in 2020, the Plan G- high deductible. These are not as high of coverage as you would get with the Plan F or Plan G or Plan N or any of the other more popular supplements, but you still have the freedom to go to any doctor in any state and any medical facility that accepts Medicare. Your cost is typically under $100 a month and, depending on where you live, it could actually be as low as $50.00 a month or even lower, I should say. So, you can check on that. We actually have an entire video on Medicare Plan F - high deductible. You might want to look at it if you're in that situation. But I would only suggest it if your finances really dictate that decision. The next most common question we get is; Which state do I use for as resident? I spent eight months in Pennsylvania and only four months in Florida. Does that make Pennsylvania my residence state? Or I spend six months and one and six months in the other. Well, the answer is that on all of these contracts, on all insurance contracts it says you put your permanent address. The definition of "permanent address" is actually written into the Social Security law. That's where Medicare laws are written, in with Social Security. Your "permanent address" is where you vote and pay taxes. It should also be the same address you have on record at Social Security. It doesn't matter if you spend only two months in Florida. If Florida it is your permanent address, that's where you have to buy your insurance policies from. This includes your prescription drug Part D, your supplement, or anything else Medicare related. It doesn't matter how much time. It matters where you vote and where you pay your taxes. Of course we also get then; what if I, you know, cheat a little? Well, this a contract. On a contract you have to write down the information and sign your name. If you're untruthful on a contract, then like any other contract, it could be voided. Insurance companies are not going to check your residence state when you sign up, apply and get accepted. But they have written into every single one of these policies that they have the right to check if something happens. So if you have a medical event, you're in the hospital the insurance company is looking at hundreds of thousands of dollars in costs, they have the right to check to make sure that everything is true on your application. If they find it wasn't truthful, they have the right to void the contract. That is a decision that can be made, and of course it would be made when you are an absolute worst circumstances. I look at insurance is it's, it's... what we use to mitigate financial risks. Well if you're not truthful on the application then you're not mitigating financial risk. You're just changing it from one scenario to another. That's not a wise decision. The next question would be; what if you have Medicare and you decide to move? You decide to change your permanent resident address. What happens? Well, first off if you have a Medicare supplement, that is good in every single state. It's good every where you go. You do not need to do anything. If you have a Medicare supplement and there's the exact same supplement, offer by the exact same company in a different state then ... that company has the right to raise your price if it's a higher price in that state. You have the right to ask for a lower price if the supplement is a lower price in that state. The prices in every state are different. If you have questions on that, contact us will be able to give you some more specific information. But keep in mind a lot of these companies have one subsidiary working in one state another subsidiary working in another. Those are not the same policies. policies. If they don't have the same policy, they can't do anything with the price. Let's say you have your prescription drug Part D plan, or you happen to have a Medicare Advantage plan. Then what will you do? Well first off, when you're going to move, when you know you're going to move you need to notify your prescription drug plan or your Medicare Advantage plan company. Typically do so 30-days before the month that you're going to move. So, the month before the month you're going to move. Call them up, tell them you're going to move out of their service area. Our new residence is going to be such and such. They will send you a letter. In that letter they're going to say "you're moving out of our service area so we can no longer cover you." It is called a disenrollment letter. That's your free pass. With the disenrollment letter, once you've moved or even as you're planning to move you can then get a new prescription drug plan or new Medicare Advantage plan for the location that you've moved to. You don't have to wait for a annual enrollment , you don't have to wait for anything. It is a special enrollment period for you. You simply apply to the insurance company for a new plan. They will want to see typically the disenrollment letter. That's what gives you the right to get the new plan. No one can deny you coverage. You will simply have a new plan with the new address for where you are now living. One of the best sources for taking a look at and researching your Medicare rights Not just the rights for when you're moving, but then when you can have a special circumstance that will give you the right to purchase another plan. We prefer the "Choosing a Medigap Policy" guide. Link Below. When you look on pages 22 and 23 of the 2016 version could be different pages on another version when Medicare puts out the 2017 and 2018 .. There is goin to be a table listing your rights and under what circumstances you can get different plans. Very easy to follow. Its actually great information to have for anyone who's on Medicare just to have it as a resource. So that's it! Those are our most frequent questions for Snowbirds, RV'er and some people are moving from one state to another and how it impacts your Medicare. I hope you found this information useful. - Top 5 Real Estate Markets for Snowbirds in 2024: Kind: captions Language: en Top five real estate markets for snowbirds in 2024. For decades, the seasonal migration of snowbirds, individuals escaping cold northern climates for warmer destinations has been a fixture of American life. While Florida and Arizona have long been popular choices, shifting demographics and affordability are driving interest in other emerging markets. Here's a breakdown of five key cities offering attractive real estate options for those seeking a winter escape. Delray Beach, Florida. Vibrant lifestyle, premium costs, Delray Beach presents a lively option for snowbirds seeking upscale amenities and entertainment. The average home value currently stands at approximately $338,995 with a cost of living index of 114.1, slightly above the national average. The city boasts a robust nightife, regular festivals, and a wide range of housing options from condos to luxury single family homes. The area also benefits from convenient access to three major airports within a 45minute drive, making travel easy for retirees. The proximity to numerous 55 plus communities featuring golf courses and social activities is a major draw. El Paso, Texas. Affordable sunshine and growing appeal, El Paso emerges as a compelling choice due to its affordability. The average home value is $231,886 with a cost of living index of just $81.9 significantly lower than many other warm weather destinations. Beyond price, El Paso offers a dry climate, mild winters, and a robust healthcare infrastructure. Cultural attractions, golf courses, and outdoor recreation contribute to a high quality of life. This city is quietly gaining traction among snowbirds seeking a balance between comfort and value. Pasigula, Mississippi Gulf Coast living on a budget for those prioritizing affordability, Pasigula provides an attractive coastal option. Average home values are around $143,72 with a cost of living index of 83.5. Property taxes are generally lower than in Florida or Arizona. The Gulf Coast city offers a relaxed lifestyle centered around waterfront activities like fishing and boating. While less flashy than some competitors, Pasigula delivers a practical solution for snowbirds seeking a peaceful retreat without the high price tag. Ports St. Lucy, Florida. Modern retirement communities. Portsent Lucy located on Florida's Treasure Coast is experiencing a boom in modern 55 plus communities. The average home value is $381,910 with a cost of living index of 98.6. These aren't traditional retirement havens. Many communities now include cooking classrooms, art centers, fitness rooms, and notably extensive pickle ball facilities. The ease of meeting new people is a strong appeal for those relocating from elsewhere. Yuma, Arizona. Sunshine and stability, Yuma remains a perennial favorite among snowbirds, boasting over 300 days of sunshine annually. Average home values are $298,369 with a cost of living index of 91. Unlike some overheated Arizona markets, Yuma has avoided extreme price surges. Accessible health care, a strong veteran presence, and proximity to larger medical centers in Phoenix and San Diego add to its appeal. This makes it a realistic option for those seeking a warm, stable retirement destination. Conclusion: The Snowbird Market is evolving with affordability and lifestyle amenities increasingly influencing decisions. While Florida and Arizona remain popular, cities like El Paso, Pasigula, and Portsent, Lucy are gaining traction as viable alternatives, offering a mix of sunshine, cost savings, and community.

They want minimal&nbsp;winter maintenance. No emergency phone calls ideally, and insurance that's easy to manage, and the peace of mind while they're away. Now, with that in mind, let's&nbsp;start with the best options for all those things. #1 is townhomes. This is the&nbsp;snowbird sweet spot. You still feel like you have a home, but without the full responsibility of a detached&nbsp;house. They're especially popular in places like&nbsp;Predator Ridge, The Seasons that we already&nbsp;talked about, Okanagan Landing developments. #2 is condos. And condos are the best&nbsp;for maximum simplicity. If you want maximum&nbsp;lock-and-leave living, condos are hard to beat. The pros are the building maintenance&nbsp;things are handled, snow removal is covered,&nbsp;on-site management is super easy, and&nbsp;there's security monitoring. The cons,&nbsp;though: strata rules vary, might not be something&nbsp;that you want. Storage can be limited. The key&nbsp;here is to choose a well-run building with&nbsp;clear strata policies for extended absences. Now, #3, let's talk about bare land&nbsp;strata properties. That's another excellent&nbsp;snowbird option, a very popular one, and they work&nbsp;well because you own your own home and the land,&nbsp;but there's not a lot of headaches. Often&nbsp;landscaping and snow removal are included,&nbsp;and often you get ground-level living&nbsp;as well. Often located in quiet,&nbsp;adult-oriented communities, maybe gated, and they're really popular with retirees, snowbirds especially, who want the one-level&nbsp;living and buyers downsizing from larger homes. The other option is a detached home, but proceed&nbsp;with caution. They aren't automatically wrong&nbsp;for snowbirds, but they do require planning. Challenges include things like snow removal. You’ve got to figure that out. Winter property&nbsp;checks, insurance restrictions. Normally,&nbsp;you have to have someone come and check&nbsp;once every few days. What about emergency&nbsp;response while you're away? Detached homes&nbsp;only work well for snowbirds if they're in&nbsp;a managed or gated community and you have&nbsp;reliable local support and the maintenance&nbsp;is simplified. Otherwise, they can quickly&nbsp;become a pain in the butt. Very stressful. Now, what home types should snowbirds avoid? These are the homes that cause the most problems: large detached homes with no strata, acreages&nbsp;or rural properties, especially if you have&nbsp;animals. What do you do then? Long private&nbsp;driveways, potentially steep ones. You have&nbsp;to keep those clear, maybe for the mail carrier or&nbsp;somebody coming to the door, they slip and fall,&nbsp;and all of a sudden you get sued. That's a drag. - Pros And Cons Of Buying A Home Over The Winter: Kind: captions Language: en LOOKING FOR A CHANGE OF SCENERY NOW MIGHT BE THE PERFECT TIME TO RELOCATE. NORMALLY AS WE HEAD INTO WINTER WE START TO SEE THE SLOWER MONTHS OF REAL ESTATE SALES BECAUSE IT USUALLY JUST ISN'T ON OUR MINDS-- WITH THE HOLIDAYS AND ALL. BUT IT'S EXACTLY THAT REASON THAT NOW MIGHT BE THE BEST TIME TO BUY A HOME FOR A DECENT PRICE. THESE MONTHS ARE GENERALLY KNOWN AS BUYER'S MONTHS. PEOPLE WHO ARE PUTTING THEIR HOME UP FOR SALE NOW TEND TO HAVE A SPECIFIC REASON-- LIKE A NEW JOB-- THAT'S FORCING THEM TO MOVE. WHICH MEANS A BUYER MAY HAVE THE UPPER HAND IN NEGOTIATING A PRICE. PLUS, WITH A DROP IN TEMPERATURE AND SNOW, THE WINTER CAN PROVIDE BUYERS A DIFFERENT PERSPECTIVE OF THE HOME THAN WHEN SHOPPING DURING WARM, SUNNY DAYS. "DOES THE BASEMENT FLOOD? IS THE ROOF LEAKING? IS IT SUNNY AT THE HOUSE, OR ARE THERE PATCHES OF THE GRASS THAT ARE STILL COVERED IN SNOW THREE MONTHS LATER? IT'S A REALLY DIFFERENT VANTAGE POINT THAN LOOKING WHEN IT'S SUNNY AND THE TREES ARE FULL AND THE FLOWERS ARE BLOOMING." BUT DAVE, SERESE KEEP IN MIND THAT TRYING TO BUY IN THE WINTER HAS ITS DOWNFALLS. BECAUSE PEOPLE AREN'T THINKING ABOUT SELLING EITHER-- THE NUMBER OF HOUSES ON THE MARKET TENDS TO BE LESS THAN HALF OF WHAT IT IS DURING SPRING AND SUMMER. REPORTING LIVE, JJ, WOWT -6- NEWS.

- How to House: 7 Money-Saving Tips for Winter | HGTV: Kind: captions Language: en [music playing] - Hey, guys. It's Ivy from How to House. I don't know about you, but after a long hot summer, I'm ready for some cooler weather and cozying up near a fire. But what I'm never ready for is the crazy expensive electricity bills that sometimes come with the winter weather. So here is seven steps to save you money all season long. First up is the front door or really, any door that leads to the outside. Drafts can add up to 30% to your winter utility bill so using a handy dandy draft stopper is an easy and super inexpensive way to save you in the long run. Tip number two is all about ceiling fans. And I know what you may be thinking, fans? Why would you use fans in the winter? Well, hear me out. Running your fans clockwise at a low speed will gently draw up the cooler air from the floor, and push the warm air down from the ceiling. This circulates the warm air, giving you a more comfortable temperature. Next up is windows. Now we've all been standing near a window in the winter and can physically feel the cool air coming in. Meet window shrink wrap. This insulating plastic easily adheres to your windows, and can save you big bucks on those heating bills. And when the warmer weather creeps back in, simply remove it. Now to your water heater. Simply by lowering its temperature to 120 degrees, it can reduce water heating costs up to 10%. Moving on to the furnace. It has a filter, and it needs to be checked every month. You can probably get away with only changing it every three months, but it's especially good in the winter to make sure it doesn't need to be changed more frequently to keep your house running more efficiently. These next two tips may seem like common sense, but we can't forget about them. First, the thermostat. 50% to 70% of your bill goes towards heating so every degree you lower the temperature, is money in your pocket. So make sure to turn your heat down when you're not home for long periods of time. Last, but certainly not least, bundle up and get cozy. Putting on a heavy blanket can add up to four degrees of warmth. What things you do warm without breaking the bank? Be sure to let us know in the comment section below, and don't forget to like, and subscribe. I'll see you next time.

- Florida’s EMPTY! Millions of Canadian Snowbirds Just Vanished in 2025: Kind: captions Language: en It's no secret Canadian snowbirds haven't been flocking to South Florida lately. President Trump's immigration rules and tariffs have caused controversy in Canada. >> CBS News Miami Scout joins us live from Fort Lauderdale Beach. >> I don't know if it's the hurricane, the economy, a mix of the two, but we've definitely been uh everybody around here that I spoke to has been down about 30% this year. Some Canadian snowbirds are packing up and saying goodbye to their US homes for goods. They want to sell and they want to cash out and bring back their money to Canada. >> All right. Can someone explain to me what the hell is going on with downtown Orlando? I'm someone that's from downtown St. Pete and this is literally like a dystopian hellscape. Like there is absolutely nothing going on here. I mean, what's the deal? What's the deal, guys? >> They're gone. All of them. Millions just vanished. Florida's winter beaches are eerily silent this year. Once teeming with laughter, lawn chairs, and the smell of sunscreen, they now sit abandoned. And not because of a storm or pandemic. For over 50 years, Canadian snowbirds, retirees escaping the northern cold, have flocked south to the sunshine state. But this winter, something unprecedented is happening. >> But it's been really good. So, it's very busy. So that's everywhere that we've gone and really really >> those familiar red and white license plates from Ontario and Quebec. They're nowhere to be seen. Hotels are reporting sudden drops. Restaurants once packed with seasonal regulars now serve half empty dining rooms. So what's going on? Why are millions of Canadians abandoning Florida, not just for this year, but possibly for good? Is it the rising insurance rates, the growing political tensions? Or could it be a fundamental shift in the way the next generation thinks about retirement and travel? Today, we're diving deep into this vanishing act and why it matters not just for Florida, but for the entire US winter economy. Part one, the snowbird legacy. For generations, Florida has been more than just a vacation spot for Canadians. It's been a winter ritual, a second home, a warm escape from the bitter cold of the north. They're called snowbirds for a reason. Each year from late October through early April, they migrate south by the millions. You'd see them cruising down I 95, flying into Fort Lauderdale, or pulling up to pastel condos in Naples and Sarasota. By the peak of the season, some neighborhoods in South Florida had more Canadian flags than American ones. But their impact isn't just cultural, it's deeply economic. In the past few years, over 3 million Canadians made the journey annually. That's not a vacation trend. That's a cornerstone of Florida's seasonal economy. They spent billions on short-term rentals, local clinics, car rentals, restaurants, and retail. From snowbird real estate deals to medical tourism, entire industries grew to serve this community. And for many Canadian retirees, Florida wasn't just a trip. It was a lifestyle. They bought homes, joined golf clubs, built friendships that lasted decades. In some beach towns, Canadian buyers made up nearly half of all foreign real estate transactions. This was a symbiotic relationship. Florida offered sun and snowbirds brought stability. But in 2025, that cycle is breaking and the silence along the shoreline is just the beginning. Part two, Trump's 2025 tariffs and the Canadian boycott. Well, two for sale signs south of the border now in what is increasingly becoming a trend amid growing political tensions and economic uncertainty. Some [music] Canadian snowbirds are packing up. >> But the sudden disappearance of snowbirds isn't just about weather or retirement trends. It's also political. In early 2025, the United States under the new Trump administration reignited trade tensions with Canada. A sweeping package of tariffs was introduced. 25% on Canadian steel, aluminum, and several agricultural exports. The move sparked outrage across the border. >> We will stand up for Canadian workers. We will stand up for Canadian industries, and more Canadians will stand up for each other. We should be uh doing more together, not fighting with each other, but Canadians will stand up strongly and firmly if we need to. Canadian media lit up with headlines and social media platforms turned into battlegrounds. But what followed was unexpected. This time the response wasn't just diplomatic. It was personal. Canadians began rethinking their travel plans. Columnists called for economic patriotism, urging citizens to keep their dollars at home. Provinces like Ontario and British Columbia quietly pulled advertising budgets from US-based tourism campaigns. Influencers started tagging their beach photos with Bypass the South and Travel Canada First. And slowly but surely, the ripple effect reached Florida. Bookings dropped. Vacation home inquiries slowed. Longtime snowbirds began questioning whether they were welcome or just convenient cash flow. Because for many, Florida no longer felt like a friendly neighbor. It felt like a transaction, one strained by tariffs, tension, and a growing sense of resentment. And when trust breaks down, so do traditions. This wasn't just a seasonal dip. >> A lot of them will not come because of Trump and what was going on with Canada. Uh they just don't want to come and spend their money here. Um the cost of living here has changed a lot. >> It was the beginning of a quiet boycott. Not by decree, but by decision. Millions of them, Florida's empty Canadian snowbirds, just vanished. But it goes even deeper than politics. They aren't just skipping a vacation this year. They're selling their Florida homes and walking away for good. Part three, the cost of sunshine. The Florida dream has always come with a price tag. >> We're seeing a decline in the Canadian traveler to our hotel. So, we are really trying to encourage people to come to Fort Lauderdale and the beautiful beaches. >> But in 2025, that price has skyrocketed. And for Canadians, it's become almost unbearable. Let's start with the exchange rate. The Canadian dollar has slipped to around 68 cents on the US dollar. That means every Florida purchase, from groceries to gas to golf fees, costs over a third more than it did just a few years ago. What was once an affordable escape has become a luxury retreat. But the real financial storm, home insurance. In the wake of back-to-back hurricane seasons, Florida's insurance market is in crisis. Premiums for coastal properties have more than doubled since 2023. Some Canadian homeowners now face annual bills as high as $7,000 just to ensure a modest condo. And that's if they can even get coverage. Many insurers have pulled out of high-risk areas entirely. For retirees on fixed incomes, this isn't just inconvenient, it's unsustainable. Across the Gulf Coast, real estate agents report a sharp rise in listings from Canadian owners. And these aren't strategic investments being flipped. They're homes. Places filled with decades of memories, family visits, and winter traditions. But now, those traditions are being cashed out and often at a loss. Some sellers are accepting offers 20 to 30% below market value just to get out from under the burden. One agent said it best. They're not moving for profit. They're moving for peace of mind. Because in 2025, sunshine costs more than ever. And for many snowbirds, the warmth just isn't worth the wait. Part four. When nature says no. As if soaring costs weren't enough. Nature had its own message for Florida's seasonal residents. In August of 2025, Hurricane Milton slammed into the Gulf Coast with devastating force. It was a category 4 storm that ripped through Venice, Bradenton, and parts of Sarasota. Areas long favored by Canadian snowbirds. Condo buildings were flooded. Beaches were eroded. Infrastructure from boardwalks to electrical grids was left in ruins. And while rebuilding has begun, the scars remain both physically and emotionally. For many snowbirds, returning to Florida now comes with a new sense of risk. Evacuation plans, power outages, flooded streets. These aren't vacation memories. They're warnings. Warnings that next time could be worse. Vacation rentals in these regions have taken a major hit. Hosts report that more than one quarter of their Canadian guests canled bookings or push trips indefinitely. And for older retirees, some in their 70s or 80s, the idea of navigating another hurricane season is simply not worth it. One couple from Montreal told their property manager they'd rather spend the winter in Arizona. Even if it means fewer beaches, at least we sleep better. The emotional toll is just as heavy as the financial one. Because when a place that once symbolized peace and safety begins to feel unpredictable, even dangerous, it stops being home. And this shift from sunny refuge to climate risk zone is pushing snowbirds to rethink not just their travel plans, but their long-term futures. Part five, the generation gap. But even if insurance rates drop and hurricanes quiet down, [clears throat] there's another shift Florida can't ignore. It's generational. The snowbird lifestyle that once defined retirement is no longer a dream for younger Canadians. It's a relic. In a national survey conducted last year, nearly 3/4ers of Canadians under the age of 40 said they have no interest in owning property abroad. Not in Florida, not anywhere. Why? Because for millennials and Gen Z, freedom matters more than foundations. They want mobility, not mortgages. Digital nomads, remote workers, and minimalists are reshaping how and where people spend their winters. They're choosing cities like Lisbon, Medí, or Tulum. Places with culture, co-working spaces, and shorter term commitments. To them, being locked into a condo in Clear Water isn't a dream. It's a liability. And it's not just about finances. It's about values. Today's younger travelers prioritize sustainability, experience, and flexibility over stability and tradition. Where previous generations saw Florida as the finish line, the next generation doesn't see it at all. So even as older snowbirds slowly step away due to storms, politics, or rising costs, there's no new wave waiting to take their place. And that may be the biggest shift of all. So yes, Florida's empty. Canadian snowbirds just vanished. But this isn't a fluke. It's a signal. A convergence of forces, economic pressure, political friction, climate anxiety, and cultural evolution. All colliding in one of America's most iconic states. For decades, snowbirds brought stability to Florida's volatile economy. They weren't just visitors. They were seasonal residents, investors, and neighbors. Now, with millions pulling back, entire sectors feel the vacuum. Restaurants once booked solid are cutting hours. Rental properties sit vacant and local governments are beginning to feel the budget strain from lost tourism revenue. This isn't just about fewer plane tickets sold or condos listed for sale. It's about a deeper question. What happens to a seasonal economy when the season doesn't come? Florida is being forced to adapt because the long-held formula sun plus Canadians equals prosperity may no longer apply. And if that formula is broken, the entire model needs rethinking. Not just in Florida, but in every corner of the US that's built on migration, retirement, and tradition. This is more than a tourism trend. It's the quiet unraveling of a relationship that once seemed unshakable. And what replaces it? That's the question no one can afford to ignore. If Canada, America's closest neighbor, most loyal visitor, and quiet economic partner, is stepping away, what does that say about the bigger picture? This isn't just a Florida story. It's a warning shot for every state that relies on seasonal traffic, real estate, and tradition to keep local economies afloat. Because when old habits break, they don't always come back. The question now isn't just where did they go. It's who's coming next. Florida's And what's left behind is more than empty chairs and quiet beaches. It's a blank page. One that local leaders, businesses, and future generations will have to rewrite. Thanks for watching. If you've noticed changes in your area, or if you're one of the millions rethinking where you winter, let us know in the comments. And don't forget to like, share, and subscribe for more stories that go beyond the headlines.
